1:
‘When the morning was come, all the chief priests and elders took counsel against Jesus to put him to death.’ The witnesses agree this was a formal daybreak session to ratify the night’s illegal trial, and most stress the haste of malice and the loss of the Jews’ power of execution.

The grammatical-historical commentators set the scene. Barnes and Robertson explain that after dawn the Sanhedrin held a second, ratifying meeting (Luke 22:66), since the Jews could no longer inflict death (the power having been taken by Rome) and needed a civil charge to bring before Pilate. Lightfoot lays out how the whole proceeding violated their own canons — capital causes were to be tried by day, not on the eve of a feast — so that ‘there is need of night and darkness’ for this judgment. Ellicott notes the Jewish rule that sentence was not given at the same sitting as the trial.

The fathers probe the malice. Jerome marks the ‘evil zeal’ of priests who watched the whole night for this murder; Chrysostom observes they put him to death openly, to destroy his reputation. Origen sees them binding ‘Jesus, who looses them that are bound’ (Isaiah 61:1).

The Puritan Henry and Reformed Calvin draw the point. Henry notes that, the power of capital punishment having been taken from the Jews about this time, they must deliver Christ to Pilate to execute their sentence. Calvin observes that they keep ‘the form of judicial proceedings,’ running to Pilate at sunrise that they might not be suspected of tumultuous haste, though their feet ‘ran to evil.’ The shared note is that the rulers, having predetermined his death, now hurry through the forms of law to make Rome the executioner of their malice. Lightfoot enumerates the irregularities — a capital cause judged by night, on the eve of a feast, contrary to their own canons — while Henry observes that the Sanhedrin could only ‘show their teeth,’ the power of death being taken from them. The witnesses agree that the daybreak council was a hurried formality to clothe a foregone resolution in the colour of law.
2:
‘And when they had bound him, they led him away, and delivered him to Pontius Pilate the governor.’ The witnesses note the binding and assemble what is known of Pilate, the Roman procurator into whose hands the priests pass their prisoner.

The grammatical-historical commentators supply the history. Barnes, Clarke and Robertson explain that the binding was likely a re-tying of the hands; Jesus had been bound in the garden and before Annas (John 18:12), perhaps loosed before Caiaphas, and now bound again to be led as a condemned malefactor. Ellicott gives the fullest sketch of Pilate — of the gens of the Pontii, the cognomen Pilatus (‘armed with the javelin’), appointed procurator of Judea under Tiberius, residing usually at Caesarea (Luke 2:2). Clarke adds that Pilate, after cruelties against the Samaritans, was deposed and exiled, and died by his own hand.

The Wesleyan Wesley and Reformed Gill note the purpose of the binding — for security as he passed through the streets, and for his greater reproach, that he might at once be taken for a malefactor.

The Catena entry under Cyril of Jerusalem is on point for the Passion, applying the prophecy of one ‘led as a present to the king’ to the sending of Jesus to Herod (Luke 23:7), and noting that Pilate and Herod, before at enmity, were that day made friends. The shared, dominant note is that the bound Christ is handed from the ecclesiastical to the imperial power, the priests now seeking to make the Roman governor the instrument of their will, so that the Lord of all stood, fettered as a criminal, before the bar of a profane judge. Henry sketches the Roman governance of Judea under the procurators, and Robertson notes that what the priests had done was ‘all a form and a farce,’ for they had no power to execute and now enjoyed the spectacle of the bound Christ. The witnesses agree that he who had made the Sanhedrin cower in the temple was handed, fettered as a condemned criminal, to the imperial power. Cyril of Jerusalem (on point) notes that Pilate and Herod, before at enmity, were that very day made friends through the sending of Jesus between them.
3:
‘Then Judas, which had betrayed him, when he saw that he was condemned, repented himself.’ The witnesses agree this ‘repentance’ was remorse, not saving repentance, and several ask what Judas had expected.

The grammatical-historical commentators dwell on the word. Barnes, Ellicott and Robertson stress that the Greek (metameletheis) means ‘regret’ or a ‘change of feeling,’ not the saving metanoia; the same sorrow Peter felt, but where Peter’s ‘led back to Christ… Judas had only remorse that led to suicide’ (compare 2 Corinthians 7:9-10). Barnes and Ellicott raise the question whether Judas had looked for another result — expecting Jesus to deliver himself by a miracle — though both think simple remorse a sufficient explanation. Clarke draws a providential lesson: Judas’s confession rescued Christ’s character ‘from all reproach,’ depriving infidelity of the cry of ‘imposture.’

The Reformed Calvin holds that Judas ‘not that he reformed, but… the crime which he had committed gave him uneasiness’; God ‘frequently opens the eyes of the reprobate’ to feel their misery without leading them to true reformation. Wesley adds that Judas probably thought Christ ‘would have prevented’ the outcome by a miracle.

Cyril of Jerusalem (on point) shows the thirty pieces foretold in Zechariah 11:13. The shared, dominant note is that Judas’s belated horror, sharp as it was, was the sorrow of the world that worketh death, not the godly sorrow that leads to life; seeing his Lord condemned, he was overwhelmed by a guilty conscience that drove him not to the Saviour but to the gallows. Origen marvels that the seed of virtue implanted in Judas led him to acknowledge his sin, though he cherished it not; and Barnes notes that, had Judas been a true penitent, he would have fled to the Saviour, whereas his remorse ‘led away from the Saviour to the gallows.’ The witnesses agree his anguish, real but graceless, was the sorrow of the world that worketh death. Cyril of Jerusalem dwells on the exactness of the prophecy that named not ten nor twenty but thirty pieces of silver as the price. Gill marks the change of description, that Judas, before spoken of as he who should betray him, is now named as he who had done the deed; the witnesses agree his belated horror, sharp as it was, drove him not to the Saviour but to despair and the gallows.
4:
‘I have sinned in that I have betrayed the innocent blood. And they said, What is that to us? see thou to that.’ The witnesses read Judas’s confession as an unwilling testimony to Christ’s innocence, and the priests’ reply as the hardness of hardened men.

The grammatical-historical commentators press the testimony. Barnes calls this confession ‘a remarkable proof that Jesus was innocent’: Judas had been with him three years and, had there been any guilt, was competent to charge it; instead he ‘fully and frankly confessed’ the innocent blood. Clarke notes the variant ‘righteous’ for ‘innocent.’ Robertson observes the Sanhedrin’s ‘see thou to it’ ignores both Judas’s guilt and their own — ‘the sentiment is that of Cain.’

The Reformed Calvin marks the priests’ stupidity: warned by the dreadful example of Judas, who not only confesses his sin but asserts Christ’s innocence, they still ‘do not think about themselves,’ their consciences seared. The Wesleyan Wesley exclaims at men who ‘could digest innocent blood’ yet scrupled to put the price into the treasury.

Ellicott feels the deeper guilt of the priests: ‘a word of sympathy and true counsel might have saved’ Judas even then, but their repulse ‘drove him back upon despair’; his confession was ‘the germ of repentance’ that found no absolver. Cyril of Jerusalem turns their words against them — if the price is polluted, the deed is polluted. The shared, dominant note is that even the traitor’s anguished testimony to innocent blood left the priests unmoved, their callous ‘what is that to us?’ convicting them of a murder Judas himself had named. Robertson notes that ‘see thou to it’ is a volitive future, an imperative by which the Sanhedrin throw the guilt back on Judas while ignoring their own; Clarke says they should at once have reversed the sentence and freed the innocent, ‘but this would have been justice.’ The witnesses agree the priests’ cold reply convicts them more deeply than the traitor whose confession they spurned. Cyril of Jerusalem turns their words against them: if the price was polluted, the deed of crucifying was polluted also, and the murderers no less than Judas must answer for it.
5:
‘And he cast down the pieces of silver in the temple, and departed, and went and hanged himself.’ The witnesses agree on the despair behind the act and devote much effort to harmonising the death with the account in Acts.

The grammatical-historical commentators handle both. Barnes and Ellicott note the word ‘temple’ here is the naos, the sanctuary the priests could enter, into which Judas hurled the money. On the death, Ellicott takes ‘hanged himself’ as accurate (so of Ahithophel, 2 Samuel 17:23), the horrors of Acts 1:18 caused by the self-murderer’s ‘want of skill.’ Lightfoot offers a bolder reading — that the devil, who had dwelt in him three days, strangled him and threw him down headlong, so that he burst asunder. Clarke uses the middle word ‘strangled’ to span suffocation by grief and hanging.

The Reformed Calvin draws the doctrine: this is ‘the price for which Satan sells the allurements by which he flatters wicked men for a time,’ throwing them into a fury so that they find ‘no consolation but in death’; though they are their own executioners, they in no way diminish the wrath of God. Gill notes Judas would neither keep the money nor carry it back.

Cyril of Jerusalem (on point) compares Gospel and prophecy on the casting down of the silver. The shared, dominant note is that the wages of treason gave Judas no relief: his ill-gotten silver flung back, he rushed from remorse to self-destruction, a warning that the sorrow of the world, untethered from the hope of pardon, ends not in life but in despair and death. Barnes notes that Judas, casting back the price of treason, ‘found, as all will find, that to cast away ill-gotten wealth will not alleviate the guilty conscience’; Gill observes he would neither keep the money nor carry it back. The witnesses agree the silver gave him no peace, and that, fleeing from remorse, he rushed to a violent and self-inflicted death. Cyril of Jerusalem compares the Gospel with the prophecy, that Judas repented and cast down the silver in the temple, as Zechariah had foretold.
6:
‘It is not lawful for to put them into the treasury, because it is the price of blood.’ The witnesses fasten on the priests’ hypocritical scruple, and the fathers draw a rich allegory from the field bought for strangers.

The grammatical-historical commentators expose the hypocrisy. Barnes, Clarke and Robertson explain that the Corban (sacred treasury) could not receive the price of blood (Deuteronomy 23:18); the rabbis who knew how to split hairs over Corban (Mark 7:11) balk at this blood-money. Clarke marvels at men who ‘adjudge an innocent man to death… without scruple,’ yet are ‘so very nice’ about a ceremonial point.

The fathers allegorise. Origen and Augustine read the potter’s field bought with Christ’s blood as the whole world, the potter as the Lord who formed our bodies of clay, and the ‘strangers’ buried there as the Gentiles who find repose in Christ’s blood (Romans 6:4). Jerome notes the Jews’ perverse temper became the Gentiles’ salvation, and Chrysostom that their own consciences condemned them in calling it ‘the price of blood.’

The Reformed Calvin presses the inconsistency: if the treasury was sacred, why did they take the money out of it, when all their wealth came from the offerings? ‘Whence came the pollution but from themselves?’ Cyril of Jerusalem turns it on them — if the price is polluted, so is the deed. The shared, dominant note is that the priests, scrupling over a ceremonial trifle while committing a murder, convict themselves out of their own mouths, their very care about the blood-money proclaiming Christ innocent. Clarke, with Quesnel, exclaims at hypocrites who break ‘the eternal laws of justice and mercy without scruple’ yet are ‘so very nice’ over a ceremonial point; Origen reads the strangers buried in the field as those who to the end are aliens from God, while the righteous are buried with Christ in a new tomb. The witnesses agree the priests’ scruple convicts them out of their own mouths. Cyril of Jerusalem presses the inconsistency: if the silver was too polluted for the treasury, then by their own confession the murder it paid for was polluted too.
7:
‘And they took counsel, and bought with them the potter’s field, to bury strangers in.’ The witnesses, chiefly grammatical-historical, explain the purchase and its providential irony, with the Wesleyans drawing the mercy hidden in it.

The grammatical-historical commentators handle the facts. Barnes and Robertson reconcile this with Acts 1:18, where Judas is said to have ‘purchased a field’: he furnished the means, the priests making the actual purchase (a man being said to do what he is the occasion of). Ellicott locates the potter’s field near the Valley of Hinnom (Jeremiah 19:2), probably a worked-out quarry, now needed to bury the Roman soldiers and strangers whom no Jew would admit to his fathers’ sepulchre, and finds a ‘subtle fitness’ — the accursed spot, bought with accursed money, for the burial of accursed strangers.

The Wesleyan Clarke and Wesley draw the mercy: ‘the very money for which the blessed Jesus was sold becomes subservient to… mercy and kindness,’ the bodies of strangers finding rest in the field bought with his price, as their souls find rest in his blood. Wesley notes the small price for so near a field, the clay being worked out.

Gill records the counsel taken before the resolution. The shared, dominant note is that the blood-money the priests would not keep was turned, by an overruling providence, into a burial-place for friendless aliens — a strange and unintended emblem, as the older writers saw it, of the rest that the price of Christ’s blood secures for the stranger and the outcast, and a perpetual monument to the deed by which he was sold. Ellicott notes that the field, in the foul Valley of Hinnom that had become the very type of Gehenna, was itself accursed, bought with accursed money, for the burial of accursed strangers; Calvin sees God’s providence turning the priests’ attempt at concealment into a perpetual memorial. The witnesses agree the blood-money became, unintended, a place of rest for friendless aliens. Gill records that the counsel was taken and the resolution reached only after deliberation, the priests careful to dispose of the money in a way that seemed honourable.
8:
‘Wherefore that field was called, The field of blood, unto this day.’ The witnesses agree the name became a standing, providential memorial of the deed, and note its bearing on the date of the Gospel.

The grammatical-historical commentators identify and locate it. Barnes and Robertson note the Aramaic Aceldama (Acts 1:19), just without the walls south of Mount Zion, called the field of blood both because bought with blood-money and (in Acts) because Judas’s blood was shed there — ‘both reasons could be true.’ Ellicott allows that two spots may have borne the name, or that Judas died in the field his money had bought, ‘dying literally in Gehenna… with none to mourn over him.’ Both note that ‘unto this day’ implies an interval between the event and the writing (compare the same phrase in Matthew 28:15).

The Wesleyan Clarke draws the apologetic point: Judas by returning the money and the priests by laying it out ‘raise to themselves an eternal monument’ — the one of his treachery, the others of their perfidy, and both of the innocence of Christ; as long as the Jewish polity lasted, the field stood as a witness.

The Reformed Calvin observes that ‘the more that wicked men endeavour to conceal their enormities, the more does the Lord watch over them to bring those enormities to light’: they hoped to bury their crime in a barren field, but God turned it to a perpetual memorial, the name given ‘by common consent, as if God had commanded’ it. The shared, dominant note is that the field’s very name preached, to every passer-by, the treachery of Judas, the guilt of the priests, and the innocence of the blood by which it was bought. Calvin observes that the field’s name was given ‘by common consent, as if God had commanded’ it, that the priests’ disgrace might be in every mouth; and Barnes notes that ‘unto this day’ marks an interval of some thirty years before the Gospel was written. The witnesses agree the very name preached the innocence of the blood by which it was bought.
9:
‘Then was fulfilled that which was spoken by Jeremy the prophet.’ The witnesses agree the quotation comes substantially from Zechariah, not Jeremiah, and the attribution is the chapter’s chief crux.

The grammatical-historical commentators canvass the solutions. Barnes notes the words answer to Zechariah 11:13, and offers that Jeremiah, reckoned first of the prophets by the Jews, may have given his name to the whole roll, or that a copyist erred. Robertson adds the parallel of Mark 1:2, where a composite quotation is referred wholly to Isaiah as the more prominent. Ellicott reviews the same theories — transcription error (against which the manuscripts witness), the Jeremiah-first order, or quotation from memory.

Lightfoot stands almost alone in defending the text: he ‘confidently asserts that Matthew wrote Jeremy, as we read it,’ against Beza and others who suspected a slip of memory or a copyist’s error.

The Reformed Calvin and the Wesleyan Wesley take it as a simple mistake: Calvin confesses he does not know how the name crept in but holds ‘the name of Jeremiah has been put down by mistake, instead of Zechariah’; Wesley says plainly the word ‘Jeremy… is evidently a mistake.’ Both insist the substance is rightly applied to Christ — the despised shepherd valued at thirty pieces, the price of a slave. Cyril of Jerusalem dwells on the exactness of the prophecy. The shared, dominant note is that, however the name be explained, the prophecy of the thirty pieces and the potter is strikingly fulfilled, the Evangelist’s concern being ‘not to pry into words and syllables, but to lay down the staple of doctrine’ (Jerome). Jerome urges that the Evangelist’s business was ‘not to pry into words and syllables, but to lay down the staple of doctrine,’ while Lightfoot stands almost alone in defending ‘Jeremy’ as the original reading against Beza and the rest. The witnesses agree that, whatever the explanation of the name, the prophecy of the thirty pieces cast to the potter was strikingly fulfilled. Cyril of Jerusalem, expounding the same prophecy, marvels at ‘how great and unerring the wisdom of the Holy Ghost’ in foretelling the exact price and its end.
10:
‘And gave them for the potter’s field, as the Lord appointed me.’ The few witnesses agree the verse completes the prophecy and underlines the providence that fulfilled it through men who meant otherwise.

The grammatical-historical Barnes expounds the original in Zechariah: the prophet, sent as a shepherd to the people, asks his price; in contempt they weigh him thirty pieces, the price of a slave, which God bids him ‘cast to the potter’ — that is, throw away. So in Christ’s day the same contempt was shown: the Messiah came, was hated and rejected, and valued at a slave’s price. Gill harmonises the wording — ‘I gave’ (as the Syriac reads) for ‘they gave’ — and explains that the money, cast down in the temple, was taken up and paid to the potter in the sanctuary, so that the prophet’s ‘in the house of the Lord’ is satisfied.

The Reformed Calvin draws the doctrine: by this clause Matthew confirms ‘that this was not done without the providence of God’; while the priests ‘have a different object in view, they unconsciously fulfil an ancient prediction,’ for they could never have thought to buy the field had not God ‘turned their blameworthy conduct’ to execute his own purpose. Wesley glosses ‘as the Lord appointed me’ as the prophet’s commission to write and record.

Cyril of Jerusalem (on point) resolves the seeming discrepancy between ‘the house of the Lord’ and ‘the potter’s field.’ The shared, dominant note is that the purchase of the field, far from a chance arrangement, was the unwitting fulfilment of prophecy: the very men who sought to bury their crime in a barren plot were, by the overruling hand of God, accomplishing the word spoken long before of the rejected Shepherd sold for the price of a slave. Barnes expounds the Zechariah original — the shepherd asking his price, weighed thirty pieces in contempt, bidden cast them to the potter — and sees the same contempt repeated when the Messiah was valued at a slave’s price. The witnesses agree the purchase of the field fulfilled prophecy by men who, with a quite different object in view, unconsciously executed the purpose of God.
11:
‘Art thou the King of the Jews? And Jesus said unto him, Thou sayest.’ The witnesses agree Christ confesses a kingship before Pilate while standing in lowly silence under his accusers, and several note John’s fuller account.

The grammatical-historical commentators frame the charge. Barnes, Ellicott and Robertson explain that the priests, knowing Pilate would not regard a charge of blasphemy, substituted the political accusation of perverting the nation and forbidding tribute to Caesar (Luke 23:2); the one question that ‘really mattered’ was the claim to kingship. Ellicott draws on John for the private dialogue in which Jesus declared his kingdom ‘not after the manner of the kingdoms of the world’ (John 18:36), which moved Pilate to seek his release.

The fathers read the two answers. Origen marvels that ‘the Judge of the whole creation… humbled himself’ to stand before an earthly judge; Hilary explains that to the high priest, learned in the Law, Christ said ‘Thou hast said,’ but to Pilate, ignorant of the Law, ‘Thou sayest,’ because the salvation of the Gentiles is through that confession. Chrysostom and Jerome note he answered Pilate, who asked in good faith, but was silent to the priests, judging them unworthy of a word.

The Reformed Calvin draws the comfort: though it was a shocking sight for the Son of God to stand bound at a profane man’s bar, ‘our salvation consists in the doctrine of the cross’ (1 Corinthians 1:23); he stood condemned that we, delivered, might approach the heavenly throne. Wesley notes Jesus avows himself a king, showing his later silence was not from fear. The shared note is that Christ openly owns a heavenly kingship even as he submits to be judged. Henry notes that Christ ‘stood as the prisoner before the judge’ that we might stand in God’s judgment, and Calvin that none need be offended at his subjection, since by it the heavenly throne is opened to us. The witnesses agree he confessed a kingdom not of this world even as he submitted to be tried by an earthly governor, his lowliness itself the ground of our deliverance.
12:
‘And when he was accused of the chief priests and elders, he answered nothing.’ The witnesses agree Christ’s silence under accusation was deliberate and prophesied, the calm of conscious innocence.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the contrast. Barnes explains that, conscious of his innocence and knowing they could not prove the charges of perverting the nation and forbidding tribute (Luke 23:2), ‘in conscious innocence, he was silent.’ Ellicott draws the picture — the ‘vehement clamour of the accusers, the calm, imperturbable, patient silence of the accused, and the wonder of the judge’ — recalling the like silence before Caiaphas (Matthew 26:63).

The Reformed Calvin resolves why the Evangelists call him silent when he had just answered: ‘he had a defense at hand, but voluntarily abstained from producing it.’ His earlier reply was not to seek acquittal but to maintain that he was the promised Redeemer; now ‘the integrity of Christ was such that the judge saw it plainly without any defense.’ Gill notes the heaping of accusations — that he stirred sedition throughout Judea from Galilee to Jerusalem.

The Wesleyan Clarke remarks that ‘an answer to such accusations was not necessary: they sufficiently confuted themselves.’ The shared, dominant note is that Christ’s silence was neither helplessness nor obstinacy but a chosen restraint: innocent and able to refute every charge, he held his peace, both because the calumnies confuted themselves and because, as the prophets had foretold of the suffering Servant, he would not open his mouth, allowing his very stillness to testify to an innocence that needed no defence. Calvin notes that Pilate wished Christ to plead, that he might be acquitted without giving offence, and commends so far the governor’s regard for innocence; Ellicott marks the contrast of vehement clamour and imperturbable calm that made the judge wonder. The witnesses agree the silence was a chosen restraint, the integrity of Christ being plain to the judge without any defence. Gill notes the accusations were heaped up — that he stirred sedition throughout the land — yet to all of them the conscious innocence of Christ made answer only by a dignified silence.
13:
‘Hearest thou not how many things they witness against thee?’ Only two witnesses comment, both briefly, so the synthesis is short; both read Pilate’s question as an attempt to draw out a defence from the silent prisoner.

The grammatical-historical Barnes corrects the sense: the priests ‘were not witnesses, but accusers,’ and Pilate refers to the repeated, pressing charges — that Jesus stirred up the people, ‘teaching throughout all Judea, from Galilee to Jerusalem,’ and exciting them to sedition (Luke 23:5). The Reformed Gill notes Pilate asked this ‘observing he made no reply… and in order to draw something out of him.’

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is necessarily short, but the two witnesses agree on the substance: Pilate, astonished at the prisoner’s silence amid a storm of accusations, presses him to answer, as much from his own perplexity as from any judicial need. The governor had already perceived the hollowness of the charges, yet hoped that Jesus would speak in his own defence and so give him a ground on which to acquit him.

The very form of the question — ‘how many things they witness against thee’ — betrays that the accusations were many and loud, and that Pilate marvelled a man so assailed should remain unmoved. It thus prepares for v.14, where the governor’s wonder at that continued silence is expressly recorded; and it sets in relief, as the older expositors loved to observe, the patient majesty of the Lamb who, though he could have confounded every accuser, chose to be led as a sheep dumb before her shearers, that the Scripture of the silent Servant might be fulfilled. Barnes notes the charges were repeated and pressed — that Jesus stirred up the people throughout all Judea, from Galilee to Jerusalem — and that Pilate, perplexed at the silence, sought to draw out a reply. The witnesses agree the question betrays both the multitude of the accusations and the governor’s growing wonder at the prisoner’s unbroken calm. Gill adds that Pilate, observing he made no reply, put the question in the hope of drawing some defence from him by which he might be acquitted.
14:
‘And he answered him to never a word; insomuch that the governor marvelled greatly.’ The witnesses agree Christ’s total silence amazed Pilate, fulfilling the prophecy of the silent Servant.

The grammatical-historical commentators stress the completeness of the silence. Barnes notes there was ‘no need of his replying’: innocent, unarmed, alone, with a public life well known, the charge bore ‘the aspect of absurdity’ on its face. Robertson renders the precise Greek — ‘he did not reply… up to not even one word’ — a ‘silent dignity’ that ‘amazed Pilate.’ Clarke observes that what most affected the governor was not that Jesus offered no defence but that he ‘did not return railing for railing.’

Clarke and the others point to the prophecy: ‘silence under calumny manifests the utmost magnanimity… this very silence was predicted’ (Isaiah 53:7). Where the priests were confounded by it, Pilate, having ‘no interest to serve,’ was ‘deeply affected.’ Gill notes the silence was kept even though Pilate spoke in a kind and friendly way, with a view to his good.

The shared, dominant note is that the prisoner’s unbroken silence, so unlike anything the governor had seen, was at once the proof of his innocence and the fulfilment of Scripture: as a sheep before her shearers is dumb, so he opened not his mouth. Pilate’s great wonder, the witnesses agree, was itself an unwilling testimony — the Roman judge, accustomed to the protests and pleas of the guilty, stood amazed before a meekness that answered accusation with calm and insult with patience, and so confessed, in his very astonishment, that here was no ordinary man. Barnes notes that the very appearance of Jesus — poor, unarmed, alone, his life public and well known — was itself a refutation, so that no answer was needed; Clarke marks that this silence was foretold. The witnesses agree Pilate’s great wonder was an unwilling testimony to a meekness unlike anything in his experience, which answered insult with patience. Gill notes the silence was kept even though Pilate had spoken in a kind and friendly way, with a view to the prisoner’s good.
15:
‘Now at that feast the governor was wont to release unto the people a prisoner, whom they would.’ The witnesses explain the custom and Pilate’s attempt to use it to free Jesus, with the fathers reading Barabbas typologically.

The grammatical-historical commentators describe the practice. Barnes, Clarke and Ellicott note it is the only recorded instance, of uncertain origin — perhaps a concession to conciliate the Jews under Roman oppression, perhaps borrowed from the Roman custom of releasing prisoners at a feast. It was, they agree, the passover feast that is meant.

The fathers read it as deliverance and type. Chrysostom sees Pilate contriving by this custom to rescue Christ, setting a convicted murderer beside the innocent so that the Jews ‘might not have the shadow of an excuse.’ Origen finds the people, ‘like wild beasts,’ preferring Barabbas — where Jesus is not, ‘there are strifes and fightings.’ Hilary reads Barabbas (‘son of a father’) as shadowing the unbelief to come, when Antichrist would be preferred to Christ. Leo notes Pilate, though less guilty than the Jews, ‘was not guiltless, seeing he resigned his own jurisdiction.’

The Reformed Calvin marks the ‘insatiable cruelty’ of priests and the ‘furious obstinacy’ of the people, who were not content to kill the innocent unless they also released, in his place, a wicked man. The Wesleyan Wesley fixes the custom at the yearly passover. The shared, dominant note is that the very custom designed to show mercy became the occasion of the deepest injustice, as Pilate’s expedient to free Jesus was turned by the priests into the means of his condemnation and a murderer’s release. Origen reads the freed Barabbas as the body given over to sin where Jesus is bound, and Augustine notes that Pilate ‘strove much’ to release Jesus, making many efforts which Matthew passes over in few words. The witnesses agree that the custom designed to show mercy became the occasion of the deepest injustice, Pilate’s expedient turned by the priests into the means of condemnation. Leo adds that, though the impiety of the Jews exceeded Pilate’s fault, the governor was not guiltless, since he resigned his own jurisdiction and acquiesced in the injustice of others.
16:
‘And they had then a notable prisoner, called Barabbas.’ The witnesses identify him as a notorious insurrectionist and murderer, and several weigh the reading ‘Jesus Barabbas.’

The grammatical-historical commentators describe him. Barnes and Clarke note ‘notable’ here means notorious — a leader of sedition who had committed murder in an insurrection (Luke 23:19, Mark 15:7). Clarke and Ellicott discuss the textual evidence for the fuller name ‘Jesus Barabbas’: Bar-abbas (‘son of a father’) being a patronymic, the man may have borne the personal name Jesus, which later reverence for the Holy Name would naturally blot out. Ellicott thinks it ‘considerable, though not quite decisive’ evidence.

Lightfoot supplies the rabbinic background, noting Bar-abba (or Bar-ba) was a very usual name among the Talmudists, and recalls Josephus’s account of the cry ‘the Son cometh’ (Bar Ba) during the siege.

The shared, dominant note is that the man set beside Christ for the people’s choice was the worst of malefactors — a robber, rebel, and murderer — so that Pilate, in offering them Barabbas or Jesus, expected the innocent teacher to be preferred to so infamous a criminal. The irony the witnesses feel is sharpest if the fuller name be read: the people were asked to choose between Jesus Barabbas, ‘son of a father’ and a son of violence, and Jesus called Christ, the Son of the Father and the Prince of peace; and they chose the murderer, demanding that the Author of life be crucified in his stead. Barnes and Clarke note that Barabbas had taken a prominent part with insurgents and committed murder in the rising, and that John calls him a robber. Ellicott weighs the manuscript evidence for ‘Jesus Barabbas’ as considerable though not decisive, the personal name likely blotted out by later reverence for the Holy Name. The witnesses agree the man set beside Christ was the worst of malefactors, so that Pilate reasonably expected the people to prefer the innocent teacher; the irony, sharpest if the fuller name be read, is that they chose Barabbas the son of violence over Jesus the Son of the Father.
17:
‘Whether will ye that I release unto you? Barabbas, or Jesus which is called Christ?’ The witnesses agree Pilate hoped, by setting Christ against a notorious criminal, to secure his release through the people.

The grammatical-historical commentators read Pilate’s strategy. Barnes notes that, satisfied of Jesus’s innocence (Luke 23:14), Pilate knew him popular with the people and expected they would prefer him to ‘an odious and infamous robber’; had they been left to themselves, ‘it would probably have been done.’ Robertson sees Pilate ‘catching at straws… seeking any loophole to escape condemning’ a man he deemed harmless, falling back on the passover custom. Ellicott calls it ‘all but the last attempt of Pilate to shift off… the dreaded burden of responsibility.’

The Reformed Gill notes the proposal was put to the gathered multitude, for the release was theirs to choose, and that Pilate added the name ‘which is called Christ’ perhaps thinking they would be the likelier to ask for the Messiah. Robertson adds that the Jews interpreted ‘Christ’ to Pilate as a claim to be king in rivalry to Caesar, ‘a most unprincipled proceeding.’

The shared, dominant note is that Pilate’s offer was a calculated evasion: convinced of Christ’s innocence yet unwilling to acquit him outright against the priests, he sought to make the people the instrument of release, trusting that even a fickle crowd would not prefer a murderer to the teacher they had lately hailed. The expedient, the witnesses agree, only exposed his weakness; having the power and the duty to free the innocent, he handed the decision to a mob the priests had already begun to sway. Barnes notes that, had the people been left to themselves, they would probably have asked for Jesus, and that Pilate added ‘which is called Christ’ perhaps to make them the likelier to choose the Messiah; Robertson sees him catching at any loophole to escape condemning a man he deemed harmless. The witnesses agree the offer was a calculated evasion that only exposed his weakness. Gill notes the proposal was put to the gathered multitude, since the release was theirs to choose, Pilate trusting that even a fickle crowd would not prefer a murderer to the teacher they had lately hailed.
18:
‘For he knew that for envy they had delivered him.’ The witnesses agree Pilate saw through the priests’ pretended zeal to their real motive, and that this knowledge deepened his own guilt.

The grammatical-historical commentators name the motive. Gill explains that Pilate saw, by their conduct and charges, that it was not from regard to Caesar or to justice but ‘from envy, at his popularity… the honour, glory, and applause he had among the people,’ that they had delivered him up. Barnes presses the consequence: knowing this, Pilate ‘was bound to release him’ at once and protect the innocent, though ‘the Scriptures could not then have been fulfilled.’ Robertson notes Pilate ‘knew that the Jewish leaders were jealous of the power of Jesus with the people,’ perhaps having heard of the Triumphal Entry.

The Wesleyan Clarke draws the moral: it was Pilate’s business ‘as an upright judge… to have dispersed this mob and immediately released Jesus’; and, ‘seeing malice is capable of putting even Christ himself to death, how careful should we be not to let the least spark of it harbour in our breast’ (compare Proverbs 27:4). Wesley adds the envy was ‘because the people magnified him.’

The shared, dominant note is that the spring of the whole proceeding was envy — the priests’ fear of losing influence and applause to the new Teacher — and that Pilate, perceiving it clearly, was the more guilty for not acting on what he knew. His knowledge of their malice, the witnesses agree, both condemns the accusers, whose ‘zeal’ was mere jealousy, and condemns the judge, who saw the innocence of the prisoner and the baseness of the charge, yet wanted the courage to do justice. Barnes notes that, knowing the charge sprang from envy, Pilate was bound to release him, yet thereby the Scriptures were fulfilled, the atonement made by a judge satisfied of his innocence; Wesley adds the envy was ‘because the people magnified him.’ The witnesses agree that envy was the spring of the whole proceeding, condemning accusers and judge alike. Robertson adds that Pilate, dense about much, knew well that the leaders were jealous of the power of Jesus with the people, perhaps having heard of the Triumphal Entry and the teaching in the temple.
19:
‘His wife sent unto him, saying, Have thou nothing to do with that just man.’ The witnesses agree the dream was a providential testimony to Christ’s innocence, increasing Pilate’s alarm.

The grammatical-historical commentators describe it. Barnes and Ellicott note the chair of judgment set on the Mosaic pavement (John 19:13); Ellicott observes that under the Empire governors might take their wives, and the apocryphal Gospel of Nicodemus names her Procula and calls her a proselyte — probable enough, given the Jewish influence over women of rank then. Robertson notes her message of ‘that righteous man’ and her psychical sufferings ‘increased Pilate’s superstitious fears.’ Clarke holds ‘God had appeared unto this woman, testifying the innocence of Christ.’

The Reformed Calvin argues the dream was ‘by an extraordinary inspiration of God,’ not the devil’s work: it is ‘in the highest degree improbable’ that Satan, who had inflamed the priests, would now retard the redemption of mankind; rather God took ‘many methods of attesting the innocence of Christ,’ that it might appear he suffered in our room. Gill notes Pilate was sitting on the judgment-seat, trying Jesus.

Lightfoot supplies a rabbinic parallel of a mother’s warning. The shared, dominant note is that, at the very moment of decision, God interposed a fresh witness to Christ’s innocence through the troubled dream of a Gentile woman, so that Pilate — warned by his own wife as well as by his conscience — was left wholly without excuse. The dream serves, the witnesses agree, the larger purpose that Christ should be repeatedly acquitted before being condemned, that in his undeserved death the true satisfaction for our sins might shine the more plainly. Ellicott notes that Jews of that time had gained much influence over women of rank, making Procula’s proselyte sympathies probable; Calvin insists God took many methods of attesting Christ’s innocence, that he might be seen to suffer in our room. The witnesses agree the dream left Pilate, warned by wife and conscience alike, wholly without excuse. Lightfoot supplies a rabbinic parallel of a mother’s warning to a king, that the dream of Pilate’s wife might be the better understood.
20:
‘The chief priests and elders persuaded the multitude that they should ask Barabbas, and destroy Jesus.’ The witnesses agree the people were swayed by their leaders, who worked the crowd against the very man they had lately hailed.

The grammatical-historical commentators describe the manipulation. Barnes notes the release was to be to the people, so the rulers ‘urged on the people to demand Barabbas’; the multitude, under the priests’ influence, who but lately cried ‘hosanna’ were easily moved to cry ‘crucify him’ (Acts 3:14). Ellicott pictures the members of the Sanhedrin moving through the crowd, ‘whispering… praises of the robber, scoffs and slander against the Christ.’ Robertson notes both Sadducees and Pharisees ‘took no chances,’ using all their arts.

The Wesleyan Clarke contrasts the choice — Barabbas, who had raised insurrection and committed murder, against Jesus, ‘whose voice was never heard in their streets’ but in doing good for three years and a half. Gill suggests the arguments used: that Jesus was condemned by the council as a blasphemer, and that, if released, the Romans might come and take away their place and nation.

The Reformed Calvin marks that the people ‘were not, of their own accord, hostile to Christ,’ but, ‘having sold themselves to gratify the priests,’ forgot all justice; he draws the lesson how ‘pernicious is the influence of wicked men, who can easily turn… the giddy and changeful multitude.’ The shared, dominant note is that the cry for Barabbas was engineered by the rulers: the crowd that demanded a murderer and the death of the innocent was the instrument of the priests’ envy, a sobering proof of how easily a fickle people may be led, against conscience and gratitude, into the gravest of crimes. Gill suggests the priests urged that Jesus was condemned by the council as a blasphemer, and that his release might provoke Rome to take away their place and nation; Robertson notes both Sadducees and Pharisees ‘took no chances.’ The witnesses agree the cry for Barabbas was engineered by the rulers, a sobering proof how easily a fickle people may be led against conscience and gratitude.
21:
‘Whether of the twain will ye that I release unto you? They said, Barabbas.’ The few witnesses agree the people’s choice was a fickle and instigated preference of a murderer to the Author of life.

The grammatical-historical Barnes notes the renewed question — ‘which of the two, Jesus or Barabbas?’ — put after time had been allowed in which the priests worked on the people. The Wesleyan Clarke exclaims at the ‘fickle crowd’: ‘a little before they all hailed him as the Son of David… now they prefer a murderer to him,’ and that ‘at the instigation of the chief priests’ — a warning how ‘dangerous wicked priests are in the Church of Christ,’ capable of inducing their flock to ‘prefer Barabbas to Jesus, the world to God.’

The Alexandrian Origen reads it as the divorce of the synagogue: the mother of the people, having separated from Christ her husband, receives at last the bill of divorcement, the Gentiles being called into the house in her place (compare Romans 11:11). Gill notes Pilate asked a second time, after the priests had improved the interval against Jesus.

The shared, dominant note is that the deliberate, instigated choice of Barabbas over Jesus is the chapter’s starkest reversal: a people who days before had strewn his path now, at their leaders’ prompting, demand the release of a robber and murderer and the death of the innocent. The witnesses see in it both a warning of the multitude’s fickleness when led by corrupt shepherds and, in Origen’s reading, the solemn turning-point at which the old covenant people, rejecting their Messiah, gave place to the Gentiles called in their stead. Gill notes the second question was put after the priests had improved the interval against Jesus, and Clarke warns how dangerous wicked priests are in the Church, capable of inducing their flock to prefer ‘Barabbas to Jesus, the world to God.’ The witnesses agree the instigated choice of a murderer over the Author of life is the chapter’s starkest reversal. Origen reads the moment as the synagogue’s bill of divorcement, the old covenant people giving place to the Gentiles called into the house in their stead.
22:
‘What shall I do then with Jesus which is called Christ? They all say, Let him be crucified.’ The witnesses mark the people’s turn to bloodlust and Pilate’s shirking of his own responsibility.

The grammatical-historical commentators read the moment. Robertson sees Pilate ‘pressing home the problem of Jesus with the dim hope that they might ask for Jesus also,’ but the crowd has ‘learned their lesson’; the very people who shouted ‘Hosanna’ on Sunday now shout ‘Let him be crucified,’ while ‘Pilate is shirking his own fearful responsibility… behind popular clamour.’ Ellicott notes this is the first direct demand for crucifixion, the cry coming from the multitude as the result of the priests’ prompting (v.20).

The Reformed Calvin observes Pilate resorting to a fresh expedient: he argues that Christ’s death would bring disgrace on themselves, since he was reputed their King and Christ — appealing to their honour when their compassion failed — yet even this ‘did not abate the fierceness of their cruelty.’

The Wesleyan Wesley notes they chose the very punishment Barabbas had deserved, and that, ‘in their malice they forgot with how dangerous a precedent they furnished the Roman governor,’ which within a few years ‘turned dreadfully upon themselves.’ The shared, dominant note is that the crowd’s demand for crucifixion — the punishment of the robber and rebel — reveals how completely the priests had turned the people, and how completely Pilate had abdicated his office, surrendering to clamour the man he knew to be innocent and seeking to hide his own injustice behind the voice of the mob. Robertson notes the very crowd that shouted ‘Hosanna’ on Sunday now shout ‘crucify,’ Pilate hiding his weakness behind their clamour; Calvin sees him appealing to their honour when compassion failed, yet their cruelty unabated. The witnesses agree the demand for crucifixion shows how wholly the priests had turned the people and how completely Pilate had abdicated his office. Wesley notes they chose for Jesus the very punishment Barabbas had deserved, and that in their malice they furnished Rome a dangerous precedent which soon turned upon themselves.
23:
‘Why, what evil hath he done? But they cried out the more, Let him be crucified.’ The witnesses agree Pilate’s question was at once a confession of Christ’s innocence and a confession of his own cowardice.

The grammatical-historical commentators press both points. Gill notes the question proves that ‘of all the things they had accused him, they had not… given proof of one single action that was criminal.’ Barnes records, with Luke, that Pilate put the question three times, so anxious was he to release him, proposing to ‘chastise him and let him go’ (Luke 23:22) — an expedient that ‘showed his weakness’ and only stimulated the crowd. Robertson calls it ‘a feeble protest by a flickering conscience,’ Pilate descending to argue with a mob now bent on a ‘lynching.’

The Wesleyan Clarke marks the injustice: Pilate ‘plainly saw that there was nothing laid to his charge’ deserving condemnation, yet the people, unable to answer his question, ‘are determined on his death.’ Ellicott notes the question ‘attested the judge’s conviction of the innocence of the accused, but it attested also… his cowardice.’

The shared, dominant note is that Pilate’s thrice-repeated ‘what evil hath he done?’ is an unanswerable testimony to Christ’s innocence, met not with reason but with redoubled clamour. The crowd, having no charge to prove, can only cry the louder; and the governor, convinced of the prisoner’s guiltlessness and even proposing a milder punishment, has not the courage to acquit him. His weakness, the witnesses agree, turned a judgment-seat into the scene of a mob’s triumph, justice yielding to the violence of a passionate multitude. Barnes records, with Luke, that Pilate asked three times, proposing to chastise and release him, an expedient that only stimulated the crowd; Ellicott marks the question as attesting both the judge’s conviction of innocence and his cowardice. The witnesses agree the unanswerable ‘what evil hath he done?’ was met not with reason but with redoubled clamour, justice yielding to the mob. Gill notes that of all their accusations not one criminal act had been proved, so that Pilate’s question stood as an open confession of the prisoner’s innocence.
24:
‘Pilate… took water, and washed his hands before the multitude, saying, I am innocent of the blood of this just person.’ The witnesses agree the act was a vivid but empty symbol that could not wash away his guilt.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain the gesture. Barnes, Clarke and Ellicott root it in the Mosaic rite where the elders washed their hands over an unsolved murder (Deuteronomy 21:6, Psalms 26:6); yet Barnes insists ‘the mere washing of his hands did not free him from guilt,’ for as a magistrate he was bound to free the innocent. Clarke notes he had the armed force to disperse the mob. Robertson sees it as ‘a last resort… a common symbol of cleanliness’ with ‘a slap at them.’

The Reformed Calvin likens Pilate to a sailor who, after a violent tempest, ‘gives way’: he ‘lays aside his authority as a judge, and yields to their furious outcry,’ the necessity not excusing him; ‘how could a few drops of water wash away the stain of a crime which no satisfaction… could obliterate?’ Wesley notes the custom was common among heathens and Jews alike.

Cyril of Jerusalem (on point) bids us glory in the cross, the power of God to them that are saved (1 Corinthians 1:23-24). The shared, dominant note is that Pilate’s public hand-washing, however dramatic, was a hollow evasion: he proclaimed the innocence of his prisoner and in the same breath condemned him, transferring in word a guilt he could not in fact transfer. The water left his hands as stained as before, for the judge who knows the accused is innocent and yields him to death for fear of the crowd is himself a partaker in the blood he disowns. Barnes insists the washing did not free Pilate, who as magistrate was bound to acquit the innocent and had the armed force to disperse the mob; Robertson sees in the gesture ‘a slap at them.’ The witnesses agree the water left his hands as stained as before, the judge who yields the innocent to death for fear of the crowd being himself a partaker in the blood he disowns.
25:
‘Then answered all the people, His blood be on us, and on our children.’ The witnesses agree this self-imprecation was dreadfully answered in the fall of Jerusalem, while several caution against abusing it.

The grammatical-historical commentators trace the fulfilment. Barnes and Clarke note the people invoked the guilt of his death upon themselves and their children, and that within forty years — in the siege under Titus — more than a million perished, and so many were crucified that ‘there was found a deficiency of crosses.’ Ellicott, however, observes that ‘but a fractional part of the people were present,’ and that some rulers (Joseph, Nicodemus, Gamaliel) had not consented (Luke 23:51). Robertson is emphatic that ‘this language is no excuse for race hatred today… the sins of all of us nailed Jesus to the Cross.’

The Reformed Calvin holds the Jews pronounced this curse ‘without any concern,’ their ‘inconsiderate zeal’ carrying them headlong, so that they ‘cut themselves off from the hope of pardon’; he draws the warning to ‘guard against headlong rashness in all our judgments.’ Wesley notes how literally it ‘befell some of those who now joined in this cry, as it certainly did many of their children.’

The shared, dominant note is that the people, in a frenzy of instigated zeal, called down upon themselves and their posterity the guilt of innocent blood — a self-condemnation the witnesses see solemnly answered in the destruction of the nation. Yet the more careful among them refuse to make it a charter for hatred: only a part of the people spoke it, the guilt was shared by Pilate and by sinners of every age, and the blood thus invoked in curse is the same that, sprinkled in faith, speaks better things and offers pardon to all. Barnes notes that among the Athenians an accuser devoted himself and his children to the punishment if the accused proved innocent, and that the curse came upon the Jews ‘in the righteous judgment of God’; Calvin warns against the headlong rashness that cuts men off from pardon. The witnesses agree that the blood thus invoked in curse is the same that, sprinkled in faith, speaks better things and offers pardon to all.
26:
‘Then released he Barabbas unto them: and when he had scourged Jesus, he delivered him to be crucified.’ The witnesses agree the scourging was a brutal Roman prelude to crucifixion, and several read Barabbas’s release as an emblem of the gospel.

The grammatical-historical commentators detail the scourging. Barnes, Clarke, Ellicott and Robertson explain it was the Roman flagellum — knotted thongs weighted with lead or bone, the prisoner stripped and bound to a pillar — a customary and savage preliminary to crucifixion (distinct from the Jewish forty stripes save one, 2 Corinthians 11:24). Lightfoot notes the Roman custom of scourging those to be crucified. Robertson observes Pilate ‘lost interest… when he discovered that he had no friends in the crowd.’

The Puritan Henry and Reformed Gill read Barabbas typologically: ‘he was delivered up, that we might be delivered,’ the guilty robber released while the innocent is condemned — whereas the common providence is that the transgressor is a ransom for the upright (Proverbs 21:18), here ‘the upright is a ransom for the transgressor.’ Gill makes Barabbas an emblem of the rebels for whom Christ suffered, released when he was bound.

The Reformed Calvin presses the substitution: Pilate, ‘contrary to his will,’ condemns him whom he pronounced innocent, that the Son of God ‘should be condemned in a solemn manner’ as our surety, enduring the punishment we deserved. Cyril of Jerusalem cites the prophecy ‘I gave my back to the scourges’ (Isaiah 50:6). The shared, dominant note is that the release of the guilty and the scourging of the innocent enact, even in the hands of Christ’s enemies, the heart of the gospel: the just suffers that the unjust may go free. Clarke notes the scourging was so severe that some were whipped to death, and that ‘lenity in Christ’s case is not to be allowed; he must take all the misery in full tale’; Henry sees in Barabbas released and Christ bound the great exchange. The witnesses agree the release of the guilty and the scourging of the innocent enact, even in the hands of his enemies, the heart of the gospel.
27:
‘Then the soldiers of the governor took Jesus into the common hall, and gathered unto him the whole band.’ The witnesses agree the soldiers now subjected Christ to a brutal mockery of royalty, and the fathers find in each indignity a mystery.

The grammatical-historical commentators set the scene. Barnes, Ellicott and Robertson explain the praetorium (the governor’s palace or barracks) and the ‘band’ as the cohort, a tenth of a legion (four to six hundred men); the soldiers, heathen, would enjoy showing contempt for the Jews as well as for the condemned. Augustine harmonises Matthew’s ‘scarlet’ with Mark’s ‘purple’ (Mark 15:17) as shades of one colour.

The fathers read the mockery mystically. Jerome and Remigius see in the scarlet robe the bloody works of the Gentiles or the Lord’s own blood-stained flesh, and in the crown of thorns his bearing of the Gentiles’ sins or of sinful flesh (Romans 8:3); Origen reads the reed as the broken kingdom of the world Christ took to triumph over it. Chrysostom marks that ‘not one member only, but his whole body suffered,’ the devil entering in triumph into all.

The Reformed Calvin draws the doctrine: ‘we ought to consider what we have deserved’ — our filthiness deserves that God and the angels should abhor us — ‘but Christ… resolved to be dishonoured by every kind of reproaches’ that he might present us pure, the disgrace he bore on earth obtaining favour for us in heaven and restoring God’s defaced image in us. The shared, dominant note is that the soldiers’ sport — robe, crown, reed, and homage — was a savage parody of kingship in which, unknown to them, the true King bore our shame, that those who deserved abhorrence might be clothed in his honour. Augustine notes the coloured robe denotes those heretics who said Christ had only a shadowy, unreal body, and Barnes describes the band as a cohort gathered for the sport of the scourging. The witnesses agree the soldiers’ parody of kingship was, unknown to them, the true King bearing our shame, that those who deserved abhorrence might be clothed in his honour.
28:
‘And they stripped him, and put on him a scarlet robe.’ The witnesses agree the robe was a cast-off military cloak used to mock the claim of kingship, and note the harmony of the colours.

The grammatical-historical commentators describe it. Barnes, Ellicott and Robertson identify the chlamys or paludamentum — the short cloak worn by Roman generals, officers, and emperors as a mark of military command — here, they agree, ‘some cast-off’ or worn-out cloak, possibly Pilate’s own or the one in which Herod had arrayed him (Luke 23:11). They reconcile the ‘scarlet’ of Matthew with the ‘purple’ of Mark and John (Mark 15:17), the ancient ‘purple’ covering crimson; Robertson notes the scarlet dye from the kermes insect. Ellicott records a like mockery of an idiot at Alexandria made to play a king.

The Wesleyan Clarke and Wesley note simply that they put on him a robe ‘such as kings and generals wore,’ probably ‘an old tattered one.’ Gill observes that for one man to strip another was, by Jewish canon, punishable by a fine, but the soldiers were in no such danger, and that the stripping was part of the low estate Christ submitted to.

The shared, dominant note is that the soldiers, stripping the condemned and draping him in a discarded officer’s cloak, meant only to ridicule his alleged royalty; yet the point of the mockery, the witnesses observe, lay in the truth they could not see — that the man they robed in scorn as a sham king was in fact the King of kings. The very meanness of the cast-off robe, set against the dignity of him who wore it, becomes for the older expositors a figure of the humiliation by which the Lord of glory clothed himself in our shame, that he might array us in his righteousness. Gill notes that to strip another was by Jewish canon punishable by a fine, though the soldiers ran no such risk, and Ellicott records Philo’s account of a like mockery of an idiot at Alexandria dressed as a king. The witnesses agree the cast-off cloak ridiculed his royalty, yet figured the humiliation by which the Lord of glory clothed himself in our shame.
29:
‘And when they had platted a crown of thorns, they put it upon his head, and a reed in his right hand… Hail, King of the Jews!’ The witnesses describe the cruel parody of coronation and homage.

The grammatical-historical commentators identify the objects. Barnes, Ellicott and Robertson explain the ‘crown’ was a woven wreath of some thorny shrub — Ellicott names the Zizyphus Spina Christi (the Nebk) — made to resemble the victor’s garland, and the reed a stalk of cane for a mock sceptre; the soldiers, Robertson notes, copied the salute to Caesar, ‘Hail, King of the Jews!’ Clarke and some hold the crown was meant chiefly to ridicule rather than to torture, though it caused pain. Lightfoot supplies a rabbinic parallel of an angel in Solomon’s shape struck with a reed.

Gill describes the platting of the white thorns common in Judea into the form of a crown, and the bowing of the knee in feigned worship. The witnesses agree the whole was a studied insult: every emblem of royalty — crown, sceptre, robe, and homage — was turned into an instrument of derision.

The shared, dominant note is that the soldiers’ mock coronation, designed to heap contempt on the claim of kingship, became, in the eyes of the church, the truest crowning: he who wore the thorns bore the curse of the ground (compare Genesis 3:18), and he who was hailed in scorn as King of the Jews was indeed the King whose throne is the cross. The very cruelty of the jest, the older writers saw, unwittingly proclaimed the truth: the thorns of our sin pressed upon his brow, the reed of the world’s broken power placed in his hand, that he might reign by bearing what we had deserved. Gill describes the white thorns common in Judea woven into a crown and the feigned worship of bowing the knee, and Robertson notes it was more a victor’s garland than a royal diadem, the reed a stalk of cane for a sceptre. The witnesses agree every emblem of royalty was turned to derision, yet the thorns of our curse pressed upon the true King’s brow.
30:
‘And they spit upon him, and took the reed, and smote him on the head.’ The few witnesses agree this was the climax of insult, fulfilling prophecy and marring the Saviour’s visage.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the cruelty. Barnes notes the spitting was ‘a token of the deepest contempt and insult’ (compare Matthew 26:67), and that smiting him on the head with the reed served not merely to injure by the blow but ‘to press the thorns into his head, and thus to add cruelty to insult.’ Gill, gathering the indignities, observes that what with sweat, with the blood trickling from his thorn-torn temples, and with the soldiers’ spittle, ‘his visage was more marred than any man’s’ (Isaiah 52:14).

The Wesleyan Clarke turns it to a meditation: in this condition Christ ‘preaches to the kings of the earth… that their sceptres are but reeds, with which themselves shall be smitten… at his tribunal, if they do not use them… to the advancement of his kingdom.’ Ellicott simply refers back to the spitting of Matthew 26:67.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the soldiers crowned their mockery with spitting and blows, pressing the thorns deeper with the very reed they had given him for a sceptre. The older expositors hear in it the fulfilment of the prophecies of the smitten and spat-upon Servant (Isaiah 50:6), and read in the marred face of the King a silent sermon to every earthly power: the sceptres of the proud are but reeds, and the Lord who bore their blows will one day judge those who would not bow to his kingdom. Gill gathers the indignities — sweat, blood from the thorn-torn temples, and the soldiers’ spittle — by which his visage was ‘more marred than any man’s,’ and Barnes notes the blow with the reed pressed the thorns deeper, adding cruelty to insult. The witnesses hear in it the fulfilment of the prophecies of the smitten and spat-upon Servant. Clarke turns it to a meditation, that Christ in this condition preaches to the kings of the earth that their sceptres are but reeds with which they shall themselves be smitten at his tribunal.
31:
‘And after that they had mocked him, they took the robe off from him, and put his own raiment on him, and led him away to crucify him.’ The witnesses agree the mockery ended and the procession to Golgotha began, and the fathers find figures throughout.

The grammatical-historical commentators handle the facts. Barnes and Ellicott note the restoring of his own garments and insert here John’s account of Pilate’s last appeal — ‘Behold the man!’ (John 19:5) — after which the final sentence was passed; the seamless tunic drawn again over the lacerated flesh ‘must have inflicted acute agony.’ Lightfoot cites the Sanhedrin rule that a crier went before the condemned, and the Talmudic notice of Jesus ‘hanged’ on the eve of the passover.

The fathers allegorise. Jerome notes that when scourged Christ wore the garments he took for our sins, but when crucified he resumes his own dress, and the elements are shaken. Augustine harmonises the wine mingled with gall and myrrh (Mark 15:23). Chrysostom marks that he suffered ‘without the city… that you might know the sacrifice was common’ to all the earth (compare Hebrews 13:12).

The Puritan Henry sums it: ‘Christ was led as a lamb to the slaughter, as a sacrifice to the altar,’ and bids us ‘daily take up’ our cross and follow. The shared, dominant note is that, the soldiers’ sport over, Christ was stripped of the mock robe, clothed again in his own, and led out to die: the procession to the cross in which, as the fathers saw, the lamb went forth bearing the curse, that the sacrifice offered outside the gate might sanctify a people and open to all nations the way to God. Jerome notes that when crucified Christ resumes his own dress and the elements are shaken in testimony to the Creator, and Chrysostom that he suffered outside the city to show the sacrifice was common to all the earth. The witnesses agree the lamb was led forth bearing the curse, that the offering outside the gate might open to all nations the way to God.
32:
‘And as they came out, they found a man of Cyrene, Simon by name: him they compelled to bear his cross.’ The witnesses agree the condemned bore his own cross until Christ’s weakness required Simon’s aid, and they read the Cyrenian both historically and figuratively.

The grammatical-historical commentators supply the detail. Barnes and Clarke reconcile John’s ‘bearing his cross’ (John 19:17) with the Synoptics: Jesus bore it first, then, exhausted by the scourging and the sleepless night, Simon was compelled to bear it after him. Robertson glosses ‘compelled’ (ēngareusan, a word of Persian origin, Matthew 5:41): the soldiers impress Simon as a courier, ‘Gethsemane, betrayal… scourging, have made the flesh weak.’ Ellicott, noting Mark names him father of Alexander and Rufus (Mark 15:21), infers he was ‘suspected even then of being a secret disciple.’

The Reformed Calvin sees in Christ’s weakness, bending under the cross, the proof ‘that it was a lamb that was sacrificed,’ the soldiers seizing a peasant of mean rank because the punishment was so detestable. Wesley notes he bore it himself ‘till he sunk under it.’

The fathers (carried over in the Catena) read Simon, ‘the obedient’ Cyrenian ‘stranger,’ as the Gentiles who take up the cross and suffer with Christ. The shared, dominant note is that the Saviour, worn to the point of collapse, was helped under his cross by a stranger pressed into service — a moment the witnesses read at once as a token of Christ’s true bodily weakness as the sacrificial lamb, and as a figure of the call to every disciple, and to the Gentile world, to take up the cross and follow him. Robertson notes that ‘compelled’ is the Persian word for impressing a courier, the flesh weakened by Gethsemane, betrayal, and scourging; Ellicott infers from the naming of Alexander and Rufus that Simon may have been a secret disciple. The witnesses read the Cyrenian both as proof of Christ’s true weakness as the sacrificial lamb and as a figure of the Gentiles bearing the cross. Wesley notes he bore the cross himself till he sank under it, and the fathers read Simon ‘the obedient’ Cyrenian stranger as the Gentile people made fellow-heirs by taking up the cross.
33:
‘And when they were come unto a place called Golgotha, that is to say, a place of a skull.’ The witnesses explain the name and place, and dwell on the meaning of Christ’s suffering outside the city.

The grammatical-historical commentators handle the word. Gill, Lightfoot, Barnes, Clarke and Robertson explain Golgotha (Aramaic for ‘skull,’ Latin Calvaria, whence Calvary) was probably the common place of execution, named for the skulls there, or for its skull-like shape; Robertson favours ‘Gordon’s Calvary,’ a hill north of the wall resembling a skull, against the traditional Holy Sepulchre. Ellicott notes it was ‘nigh unto the city’ (John 19:20) yet outside the wall (Hebrews 13:12), with a garden and tomb nearby (John 19:41). Lightfoot defends the spelling against Beza.

The Puritan Henry observes that ‘in the same place where criminals were sacrificed to the justice of the government… was our Lord Jesus sacrificed to the justice of God,’ the skulls there the trophies of death’s victory which Christ by dying would destroy.

The Reformed Calvin draws the doctrine from Hebrews 13:12: as the bodies of the sacrifices whose blood was carried into the sanctuary were burnt outside the camp (Leviticus 16:27), so Christ ‘went out of the gate of the city as unworthy of human intercourse,’ that, bearing our curse, he might be reckoned accursed and so atone for our sins and admit us to the heavenly kingdom. Leo, in his Passion sermon, magnifies the mystery of God’s mercy. The shared, dominant note is that the place of a skull, the public ground of death and shame, became the altar on which the Son of God was offered: cast out of the city as accursed, he sanctified a people by his blood outside the gate, turning the scene of execution into the fountain of salvation. Henry observes that where criminals were sacrificed to the justice of the government, Christ was sacrificed to the justice of God, the skulls there the trophies of a death he would by dying destroy; Robertson favours Gordon’s Calvary, a skull-shaped hill north of the wall, over the traditional site. The witnesses agree the place of a skull became the altar on which the Son of God was offered outside the gate.
34:
"They gave him vinegar to drink mingled with gall: and when he had tasted thereof, he would not drink." The witnesses harmonise the apparent discrepancy with Mark's "wine mingled with myrrh" and agree on the meaning of the refusal. The grammatical-historical commentators explain that the drink was the stupefying potion customarily given to those led to execution, "that their understanding might be disturbed" (Lightfoot, citing Proverbs 31:6, "Give strong drink to him that is ready to perish"). Barnes, Clarke and Robertson note that "vinegar" and "wine" name the same sour drink of the soldiers and common people, while "gall" (Ellicott) renders the Hebrew for "wormwood" (Proverbs 5:4; Deuteronomy 29:18) — "something at once nauseous and narcotic." Clarke and Marsh canvass the Hebrew and Chaldee words by which a translator might confuse "myrrh" and "gall," "wine" and "vinegar," but conclude the accounts are reconcilable.

On the refusal, the witnesses are united. Barnes draws the doctrine: "our Lord, knowing this, when he had tasted it, refused to drink; he was unwilling to blunt the pains of dying. The cup which his Father gave him he rather chose to drink." Ellicott reads in the mere tasting "a recognition of the kindly purpose of the act," and in the refusal "the resolute purpose to drink the cup which His Father had given Him to the last drop, and not to dull either the sense of suffering nor the clearness of His communion with His Father with the slumberous potion." Robertson agrees the women provided the drink "to deaden the sense of pain," but "Jesus desired to drink to the full the cup from his Father's hand" (John 18:11).

The Reformed Calvin distinguishes this draught from the later one of John 19:29, and rejects the view that the bitterness was itself a torment: he thinks the potion "had a tendency to promote the evacuation of blood," given "for the purpose of accelerating death," so that Christ refused it "not so much by a dislike of its bitterness as by a desire to show that he advanced calmly to death, according to the command of the Father." The Wesleyan Wesley notes Mark's myrrh-wine was "such as it was customary to give to dying criminals, to make them less sensible of their sufferings: but this our Lord refused to taste, determining to bear the full force of his pains." Ellicott adds that this fulfilled, in part, the foreshadowing of Psalm 69:21. So the witnesses agree: the Sufferer would not be drugged, but bore the cross with unclouded mind and undiminished anguish.
35:
"And they crucified him, and parted his garments, casting lots." The witnesses describe the punishment and dwell on the dividing of the garments as prophecy fulfilled. The grammatical-historical Barnes and Clarke give clinical detail: the cross laid on the ground, the body stripped and stretched, hands and feet nailed, the cross dropped into its hole with a convulsive shock, the sufferer left to die "through agony and lack of food" — "the most disgraceful and ignominious" of Roman deaths, reserved for "slaves, robbers, and the most abandoned wretches." Lightfoot notes from Jewish practice that "he was crucified naked." All agree the parting of the garments fulfilled Psalm 22:18 (which Clarke judges a later interpolation in Matthew, borrowed from John 19:24), John recording the fuller detail of the seamless coat.

The Latin fathers read the cross typologically. Augustine surveys the wood that saved through Scripture — the ark, Moses' rod that divided the sea and sweetened the bitter water, the rod that drew water from the rock, the staff that upheld his hands against Amalek — "that thus, by these steps, we may come at last to the wood of the cross." The Gloss sees in the cross's four quarters "the Church spread through the four quarters of the earth"; Raban allegorises its parts (breadth = the joy of working hands, height = looking for retribution, depth = endurance). Chrysostom notes he suffered "on a lofty cross, and not under a roof, that the nature of the air might be purified," and that the soldiers shared the garments only of "condemned persons so mean and poor as to possess nothing more."

The Reformed Calvin draws the great consolation from the nakedness: "God determined that his own Son should be stripped of his raiment, that we, clothed with his righteousness and with abundance of all good things, may appear with boldness in company with the angels." The Puritan Henry (commenting across these verses) notes that "in every minute particular of the sufferings of Christ we find some prediction in the Prophets or the Psalms fulfilled." So the witnesses gather the scene — the shameful death, the gambling soldiers, the stripped Redeemer — into one weave of prophecy and atonement: the Son of God numbered with the basest, that he might clothe the naked with his own righteousness.
36:
"And sitting down they watched him there." The witnesses comment briefly, reading the soldiers' watch both historically and with an eye to its later use. The grammatical-historical commentators explain the purpose: the four soldiers who had crucified Jesus "sat down at the foot of the cross and there watched him" (Gill), "lest his friends should come and release him" (Barnes), or, as Clarke adds, "to prevent his disciples or relatives from taking away the body or affording any relief to the sufferer." Gill notes the Roman custom of setting a guard over the crucified, "not only before they expired, but after they were dead, lest they should be taken down and buried."

Robertson catches the grim irony of the scene: the watching is "the imperfect tense descriptive of the task to prevent the possibility of rescue or removal of the body," and "these rough Roman soldiers casting lots over the garments of Christ give a picture of comedy at the foot of the Cross, the tragedy of the ages." The callous indifference of the soldiers, gambling and keeping their post while the Redeemer of the world died above them, becomes in his reading the very emblem of human blindness to the mystery being accomplished.

So the witnesses make the verse a quiet study in contrasts. The soldiers watch only to guard a corpse, suspecting nothing of what they witness; yet their very watching, the expositors note across this passage, was overruled by providence to secure the evidence of the resurrection to come, for a body so watched could not be secretly taken. The men who sat down to keep the dying Christ from rescue were, without knowing it, the first witnesses set to attest that he truly died and truly rose. Their indifference and their vigilance alike served a purpose they could not see, framing the cross with the unwitting testimony of Rome itself. The very Romans set to keep the cross thus became, in the providence the witnesses everywhere trace, the unimpeachable guarantors that the Sufferer was truly dead and that his tomb could not be secretly rifled — Rome's own soldiers conscripted, against their will and beyond their knowledge, to attest the death that grounds the gospel.
37:
"And set up over his head his accusation written, This is Jesus the King of the Jews." The witnesses treat the title — the titulus — both as the legal placard of the crime and as an unwitting proclamation of the truth. The grammatical-historical commentators explain (with Gill, Barnes, Robertson) that the inscription named "the cause of his death," written by Pilate's order and, John adds, in Hebrew, Greek, and Latin. They reconcile the four Gospels' slightly varying forms by the assumption of corresponding differences in the three languages, or of natural variation among reporters, the full inscription reading "This is Jesus of Nazareth the King of the Jews." Ellicott discerns in Pilate's chosen wording "a kind of rough tenderness towards the Man whom he had condemned," recognising his claim "to be in some sense a King," which the priests resented and vainly sought to alter (John 19:21).

The Latin fathers read the title as providential testimony. Jerome marvels that, having suborned false witnesses, "they found no other plea for putting Him to death than that He was King of the Jews." Remigius observes that "it was divinely provided that this title should be set up over His head, that the Jews might learn that not even by putting Him to death could they avoid having Him for their King; for in the very instrument of His death He not only did not lose, but rather confirmed His sovereignty." Origen sees the true High Priest bearing on his cross, in place of the plate "Holiness to the Lord," the title "King of the Jews."

The Reformed Calvin develops the providence most fully. "It did not happen without the providence of God that the death of Christ was made known in three languages." Pilate intended only "to bring reproach and infamy on the Jewish nation," but "God had a higher end in view; for by this presage he caused it to be widely known that the death of his Son would be highly celebrated, so that all nations would acknowledge that he was promised." Pilate, he says, became "a herald of Christ in the same sense that Caiaphas was a prophet" (John 11:51).
