So the witnesses agree: the mocking placard, in three tongues over the dying King, was God's own first inscription of the gospel that would be preached to all the world.
38:
"Then were there two thieves crucified with him, one on the right hand, and another on the left." The witnesses read the placing of Christ between two robbers as the depth of his shame and the fulfilment of prophecy. The grammatical-historical commentators correct the rendering: the Greek is "robbers" (lēstai), not "thieves," the same word used of Barabbas (Ellicott, Robertson), so that these "were probably members of the band of Barabbas on whose cross Jesus now hung." Lightfoot documents from Josephus the bands of robbers and sicarii who plagued Judea. Clarke notes that "thus was our Lord numbered with transgressors" (Isaiah 53:12), the Jews placing him in the midst "perhaps to intimate that he was the worst felon of the three."

The Latin fathers read the two robbers typologically. Hilary takes them to "signify that the entire human race is called to the Sacrament of the Lord's Passion," but with "a division of believers to the right, and unbelievers to the left." Leo sees "in the figure of His cross represented that separation of all mankind which shall be made in His judgment." Remigius reads them of those who strive after a strict life, "with a single intention of pleasing God" (the right) or "out of desire of human praise" (the left).

The Reformed Calvin presses the abasement and the love. It was "the finishing stroke of the lowest disgrace" that Christ was executed between robbers, assigned "the most prominent place, as if he had been the prince of robbers." Mark applies Isaiah 53:12: "he was numbered with the transgressors," for "he will deliver his people... because he will endure the punishment due to their sins." From this Calvin draws both terror and comfort: "here we perceive how dreadful is the weight of the wrath of God against sins... and we see also the inestimable love of Christ towards us, who, in order that he might admit us to the society of the holy angels, permitted himself to be classed as one of the wicked." Cyril of Jerusalem, in passing, contrasts the rejected first Church with the Catholic Church armed "by honour and dishonour." So the witnesses make the verse the very nadir of the humiliation — the sinless One ranked chief among the guilty, that the guilty might be ranked among the saints.
39:
"And they that passed by reviled him, wagging their heads." The witnesses dwell on the brutality of the mockery and on its echo of an older temptation. The Antiochene Chrysostom marks the malice: "having stripped and crucified Christ, they go yet further, and seeing Him on the cross revile Him," demanding "if thou be the Son of God, come down from the cross." He answers their taunt: "He does not come down from the cross because He is the Son of God; for He therefore came that He might be crucified for us." The Antiochene Pseudo-Chrysostom hears in their cry the very voice of their father: "the Devil said, If thou be the Son of God, cast thyself down (Matthew 4:6); and they say now, If thou be the Son of God, come down from the cross."

The Latin fathers add layers of meaning. Jerome reads "they that passed by" as those who "passed by that way and would not walk in the true way of the Scriptures," and suggests the taunt was "a suggestion of the daemons" who "felt the power of the cross." Leo rebukes them: "Where have ye ever read that the Lord came down from the cross? But ye have read, The Lord hath reigned from the tree." Hilary observes that the cross "should be an offence to all mankind, even to the faithful." The grammatical-historical Robertson and Ellicott picture "a lounging crowd, strolling from one cross to the other," wagging their heads "in mock commiseration" (Psalm 22:7; 109:25), Rulers and people alike "not ashamed to take part in the brutal mockery of a dying man."

The Reformed Calvin makes the reviling itself part of the atonement: "this certainly was more cruel than all the other tortures, that they upbraided him as one that had been cast off and forsaken by God" (Isaiah 53:4). He notes that the deepest wound to pious minds is "when ungodly men, in order to shake their faith, upbraid them with being deprived of the favor of God" — the very persecution Isaac endured from Ishmael (Galatians 4:29) — endured first by David and then by Christ, "that they might not at the present day strike us with excessive alarm." So the witnesses agree the taunts were no incidental cruelty but a renewal of the wilderness temptation and a true part of the cup, the sinless Sufferer reviled as God-forsaken that we might never be.
40:
"Thou that destroyest the temple, and buildest it in three days, save thyself. If thou be the Son of God, come down from the cross." The witnesses unfold the two-edged taunt and its echo of Eden and the wilderness. The grammatical-historical commentators note that the mockers seized on the misremembered saying about the temple (Gill, Barnes), turning into derision a word the speakers had perverted; Ellicott observes that this charge, though never formally proved, "had clearly impressed itself on the minds of the people." Robertson hears in "If thou art a son of God" the very language of the devil at the temptation (Matthew 4:3), "now hurled at Jesus under the devil's prompting as he hung upon the Cross — a pitiful picture of human depravity."

The grammatical-historical Ellicott draws out the parallel with the wilderness most fully: "Then, as now, the words 'If thou be the Son of God' were as a challenge from the Power of Evil. Now, as then, they were met by the strength of Faith. To accept the challenge would have been to show that He did not trust the Father... and therefore to disown His Sonship in the very act of claiming it." The taunt, that is, demanded that he prove his Sonship by an act that would have denied it.

The Reformed Calvin exposes the impatience of the mockers: "they indulge in a premature triumph, and do not wait for the three days" of the saying about the temple (John 2:19). They demand "such a proof of His power that, by proving himself to be the Son of God, he may cease to be the Son of God"; for Christ had come "on this condition, that, by the sacrifice of his death, he might reconcile men to God," so "in order to prove himself to be the Son of God, it was necessary that he should hang on the cross." From this he warns us "to allow God to conceal his power whenever it pleases Him, that he may afterwards display it at the proper time." So the witnesses agree: the demand "come down" was a temptation to forsake the very work of salvation, and the refusal to come down was the truest proof of who he was.
41:
"Likewise also the chief priests mocking him, with the scribes and elders, said..." The witnesses note that the rulers themselves, who should have restrained the mob, led the mockery of the dying Christ. The Reformed Gill draws out the enormity: the chief priests, "forgetting their character, office, and education, and laying aside all humanity, decency, and good manners, instead of rebuking and restraining the populace from using him in an ill manner, they themselves stood" to insult him. Having condemned him in their council and pressed Pilate for his crucifixion, they now "follow him to the cross to exult over him."

The grammatical-historical commentators identify the company and weigh a textual point. Robertson observes that "the Sanhedrin in fact" is here, for "the scribes and elders are included," and the word for mocking (empaizontes) means "acting like silly children who love to guy one another" — "these grave and reverend seniors" giving vent to their glee. Ellicott notes that "all, or nearly all, the members of the Sanhedrin who had taken part in the condemnation had come to feast their eyes with the sight of their Victim's sufferings." Clarke records that some manuscripts add "and Pharisees," but doubts the Pharisees joined, thinking Christ's recent parables "had so far convinced them that they would at least have no hand in putting him to death," so that "all the infamy of this seems to fall upon the priests, scribes, and elders."

So the witnesses make the verse a portrait of religion turned persecutor. The very men charged with teaching the people the fear of God stand at the foot of the cross like jeering children, their dignity cast off, their malice unspent even by his dying. The contrast the expositors feel, here and across this passage, is between the patient silence of the Sufferer and the unseemly glee of his judges; and the mockery of the rulers, more than that of the passing crowd, fills up the measure of the guilt the chapter records, the shepherds of Israel reviling the Shepherd of souls as he laid down his life for the sheep.
42:
"He saved others; himself he cannot save. If he be the King of Israel, let him now come down from the cross, and we will believe him." The witnesses find in this jeer an unconscious confession of the truth. The grammatical-historical Ellicott observes that "the mockers bear unconscious witness to the truth": their words "were true in a yet higher sense," for "He had come into the world to save others, regardless of Himself." Robertson agrees "the sarcasm is true, though they do not know its full significance. If he had saved himself now, he could not have saved any one. The paradox is precisely the philosophy of life proclaimed by Jesus himself" (Matthew 10:39). He adds that their promise to believe was plainly false: "they would have shifted their ground and invented some other excuse... Christ will not give new proofs to the blind in heart."

The Wesleyan Clarke turns the contradiction into the very paradox of faith: "a Savior who saves not himself, and the Son of God crucified: these are the contradictions which give offense to Jews and libertines. But a Savior who dies only to be the victim of salvation, and the Son of God who confines his power within the bounds of the cross to establish the righteousness of faith: this is what a Christian adores." Barnes notes that the time had come "for him to make atonement," and that he "chose to die for the sins of the world" rather than "work a new miracle for their gratification."

The Reformed Calvin diagnoses the root error: the Jews demand a Christ of their own imagining, "of an opposite character" to the one the prophets foretold, who represent Christ "as devoid of comeliness, afflicted, condemned, and accursed" before he ascends the throne (Isaiah 53:2; Zechariah 13:7). They acknowledge "he saved others" yet "maliciously exclude the light of God which shone in the miracles." From this he draws the lesson that "it is dangerous to depart from the word of God by wandering after our speculations," and that "the best and highest reason for believing" is "that he voluntarily subjected himself on our account to the ignominy of the cross." So the witnesses agree the taunt "himself he cannot save" was the deepest truth of the gospel: he could not save himself because he was saving us.
43:
"He trusted in God; let him deliver him now, if he will have him: for he said, I am the Son of God." The witnesses note with wonder that the priests, in their mockery, quote the very words of a Messianic psalm. The grammatical-historical Ellicott marks it: "it seems at first hardly conceivable that priests and scribes could thus have quoted the very words of Psalm 22:8, and so have fulfilled one of the great Messianic prophecies." He explains it by their refusal to read the psalm as Messianic at all, and by their very familiarity with its words, which "would naturally bring its phraseology to their lips," persuading themselves "that they were right in using it, while David's enemies were wrong." Clarke shows the quotation follows the Septuagint of Psalm 22:8, the verb "have him" rendering the Hebrew for "delight in him," and Robertson notes they add the word "now."

The Reformed Calvin reads in the taunt the sharpest of Satan's arrows. "This is a very sharp arrow of temptation which Satan holds in his hand, when he pretends that God has forgotten us, because He does not relieve us speedily." Satan "attempts to drive us to despair by this logic, that it is in vain for us to feel assured of the love of God when we do not clearly perceive his aid," and "employs his agents who contend that God has abandoned our salvation, because he delays to give his assistance." But, Calvin answers, "nothing is more unreasonable than to limit God's love to any point of time"; the delay of help is no proof of abandonment, but often "the testimony of his fatherly love" (Hebrews 12:6).

Yet there was, Calvin adds, "this peculiarity in Christ, that, though he was the beloved Son, yet he was not delivered from death until he had endured the punishment which we deserved; because that was the price by which our salvation was purchased." Hence the priests "act maliciously when they infer that he is not the Son of God because he performs the office enjoined upon him by the Father." So the witnesses agree: the mockers' own Scripture condemned them, and the very non-deliverance they jeered at was the Father's will and the world's redemption — the Son left to die because he was indeed the Son, bearing the price no other could pay.
44:
"The thieves also, which were crucified with him, cast the same in his teeth." The witnesses address the apparent conflict with Luke, who tells of one penitent robber, and they correct the harsh old rendering. The grammatical-historical Barnes offers two harmonisations: that "at first both reviled the Saviour, and that it is of this that Matthew speaks," one afterwards relenting "perhaps from witnessing the patient sufferings of Christ" — the change Luke records (Luke 23:40); or that "what is true of one of the malefactors is by Matthew attributed to both," the evangelists often attributing "to many what is said or done by single persons." He also corrects the translation: "this is a most unhappy rendering; it means in the original simply, they reproached him."

The Latin Augustine had given the classic harmonisation: Matthew "has used the plural for the singular, as in the Epistle to the Hebrews we have, Have stopped the mouths of lions (Hebrews 11:33), when Daniel only is spoken of." Since Matthew says merely "the thieves," without adding "both," "we must consider it as that common form of speech in which the singular is signified by the plural." Jerome and Chrysostom prefer the first solution: both reviled at first, but "when the sun had withdrawn, the earth was shaken, the rocks were rent, and the darkness increased, one believed on Jesus, and repaired his former denial by a subsequent confession" (Jerome); Chrysostom stresses that the robber was truly "a thief and a foe, but was afterwards totally changed," to prove there was no collusion.

The Reformed Calvin reads the reviling, by synecdoche of one for both, as the completion of Christ's reproach: "the two Evangelists had no other design than to show that Christ was attacked on every hand by the reproaches of all men, so that even the robbers who were fast dying did not spare him." So the witnesses agree the two accounts are reconcilable, and find in the verse the very bottom of the abasement — Christ reviled even by a fellow-sufferer dying beside him — while Origen notes that "the thief who was saved may be a sign of those who after many sins have believed on Christ." The same cross that drew one robber's final scorn drew the other's first faith.
45:
"Now from the sixth hour there was darkness over all the land unto the ninth hour." The witnesses agree the darkness was a supernatural sign, not a natural eclipse, and they debate its extent and meaning. The grammatical-historical and Reformed commentators stress the impossibility of an eclipse: the Passover fell at the full moon, when the moon is opposite the sun, so "it was not by an eclipse, but by the interposing power of God" (Barnes), the darkness lasting three hours. Several cite the pagan testimonies — Phlegon's report of a great eclipse and earthquake under Tiberius, and Dionysius the Areopagite's exclamation at Heliopolis, "Either God himself suffers, or sympathizes with the sufferer" — though Clarke and others weigh these critically and doubt their force.

On the extent, the witnesses differ. Origen, Chrysostom, and the grammatical-historical Barnes, Clarke and Calvin incline to a darkness "over the land of Judea" rather than the whole world; Calvin reasons that a universal eclipse "would have been regarded as natural," whereas a darkness confined to Judea, "when the sun was shining elsewhere," was the more striking miracle and judgment. Origen allegorises it: as darkness fell on Egypt while Israel had light, so "there was darkness over all Judaea for three hours, because for their sins they were deprived of the light of God," while "over the rest of the earth there is light, which everywhere illumines the Church" (Romans 11:25). Chrysostom takes it as the very "sign from heaven" they had demanded (Matthew 12:39), greater than any wrought while he walked the earth.

On its meaning, the Antiochene Pseudo-Chrysostom hears creation's protest: "Creation could not bear the outrage offered to the Creator; whence the sun withdrew his beams, that he might not look upon the crime." The Reformed Calvin gathers the views — a mark of God's detestation, the eclipse of the true Sun, the blinding of the nation — and concludes the darkness "was intended to arouse them to consider the astonishing design of God in the death of Christ," and to teach us "that the sacrifice by which we are redeemed was of as much importance as if the sun had fallen from heaven." The Puritan Henry adds that "during the three hours of darkness Jesus was in agony, wrestling with the powers of darkness and suffering his Father's displeasure against the sin of man." So the witnesses make the noonday night the cosmic sign of the wrath borne and the Sun of Righteousness withdrawing his beams.
46:
"Eli, Eli, lama sabachthani? that is to say, My God, my God, why hast thou forsaken me?" The witnesses gather at the cry of dereliction with reverent care, and they divide over how the Son could be "forsaken." All agree the words are quoted from Psalm 22:1, in the Aramaic of the country (Robertson, Clarke), and that it was "this only of the seven sayings given by Mark and Matthew" (Robertson), uttered "at the close of the three hours of darkness."

The Reformed Calvin gives the fullest and weightiest reading: this was Christ's "chief conflict, and harder than all the other tortures, that in his anguish he was so far from being soothed by the favor of his Father that he felt himself to be in some measure estranged from him." For "not only did he offer his body as the price of our reconciliation, but in his soul also he endured the punishments due to us," placed "as a guilty person at the judgment-seat of God." Yet, Calvin insists, "though he complained of being forsaken, he still relied on the aid of God"; "while the perception of the flesh would have led him to dread destruction, still in his heart faith remained firm" — proved by the very word "My God," "the shield of faith by which he expelled that appearance of forsakenness." The grammatical-historical Barnes likewise weighs the bodily anguish, "the power of darkness" (Luke 22:53), the withholding of consolation, and above all the bearing of "the curse of the law" (Galatians 3:13; 2 Corinthians 5:21) — "the manifestation of God's hatred of sin to his soul." Robertson: "Nothing from Jesus so well illustrates the depth of his suffering of soul as he felt himself regarded as sin though sinless" (John 3:16).

The Latin fathers guard against heresy. Hilary refutes those who infer that the Word was absorbed into the soul or withdrew at death: "the complaint of being deserted is the weakness of the dying man; the promise of Paradise is the kingdom of the living God." The Gloss: "God is said to have forsaken Him in death because He exposed Him to the power of His persecutors; He withdrew His protection, but did not break the union." Raban (with Damascene) takes Christ as bearing our feelings, lamenting "as representing us." The Wesleyan Clarke and Wesley press that "deity did not depart from Christ," lest the atonement lose its merit, the Godhead only "restraining so much of its consolatory support as to leave the human nature fully sensible of all its sufferings." So the witnesses, however they frame it, agree that in this cry the sinless Son truly tasted the abandonment due to our sin, yet held fast by faith to the Father he called "My God."
47:
"Some of them that stood there, when they heard that, said, This man calleth for Elias." The witnesses divide over whether the bystanders genuinely mistook the cry "Eli, Eli" for a call to Elijah, or deliberately twisted it into fresh mockery. The grammatical-historical commentators lean to honest misunderstanding. Clarke and Ellicott suppose they were "Hellenistic Jews who did not fully understand" the Aramaic, or who "imperfectly understood" the words; Ellicott adds that "there is no ground for looking on this as a wilful, derisive misinterpretation," and that "the dominant expectation of the coming of Elijah" (Malachi 4:5) "would predispose men to fasten on the similarity of sound," the strange darkness intensifying "the feeling that looked for a supernatural manifestation."

Barnes, however, reads it as deliberate derision: "this was done purposely to deride him and his pretensions to be the Messiah," for it was "the universal belief of the Jews that Elias would come before the Messiah," so the taunt mocked him "as calling upon Elias when God would not help him." The Reformed Calvin presses this view hardest: "I do not think it at all probable that they erred through ignorance, but rather that they deliberately intended to mock Christ, and to turn his prayer into an occasion of slander."

Calvin draws from it the deepest lesson of the verse. "Satan has no method more effectual for ruining the salvation of the godly than by dissuading them from calling on God"; so he "impelled the wicked enemies of Christ basely to turn his prayer into derision, intending to strip him of his chief armor." It is, Calvin says, "a very grievous temptation, when prayer appears so far from yielding any advantage that God exposes his name to reproaches." Yet he comforts the faithful: though men "change our prayers into jest, God will at length listen to our groanings." So the witnesses, whether they make the bystanders ignorant or malicious, agree on the import: the dying Saviour's cry to his God was met with a jeer about Elijah, his very prayer turned against him, that we who pray amid mockery might know our Head endured the same.
48:
"And straightway one of them ran, and took a sponge, and filled it with vinegar, and put it on a reed, and gave him to drink." The witnesses distinguish this offering from the drugged draught refused earlier, and weigh whether it was an act of pity or of mockery. The grammatical-historical commentators connect it with the cry John records, "I thirst" (John 19:28), one of the effects of crucifixion being "excessive thirst" (Barnes). The drink was "the common drink of the Roman soldiers," sour wine mixed with water, set ready in a vessel for the guard (Barnes, Ellicott). The sponge, Ellicott suggests, "had probably served instead of a cork to the jar," and the reed was, John says, "a stalk of hyssop" (John 19:29), long enough to reach the elevated mouth of the Sufferer.

Crucially, the witnesses note, "this vinegar Jesus drank, for it was not intended to stupify him, or blunt his sense of pain like the wine and myrrh" (Barnes). Robertson agrees: "Jesus did taste this mild stimulant, for he thirsted." Unlike the narcotic potion of the earlier verse, which he refused that he might bear the full force of his pains, this plain sour wine he received, fulfilling the word of Psalm 69:21.

On the spirit of the act, the witnesses lean to a rough kindness. The Wesleyan Wesley judges "it does not appear that this was given him in derision, but rather with a friendly design"; Robertson and Ellicott agree it was prompted "by a rough pity," and that "while he was doing this, the others tried to interrupt him" (the imperfect tense, Ellicott notes, implying the attempt was resisted). The Reformed Calvin allows that the vinegar may have been "repeatedly offered for the sake of annoyance," but also that this plain drink was simply held to his thirsting mouth. So the witnesses make the verse a small window of compassion amid the cruelty: one bystander, moved by pity, eased the Saviour's thirst, and the dying Christ, who had refused the drug, accepted the simple drink — taking the cup of our common suffering even as he drained the cup of our redemption.
49:
"The rest said, Let be, let us see whether Elias will come to save him." The witnesses weigh whether these words breathe derision or a half-superstitious expectation, and they note a textual addition some manuscripts make here. The grammatical-historical Barnes reads it as scorn: "still deriding his sufferings, and refusing to allow even the poor consolation of a drink, to assuage the thirst of the Saviour of the world in his dying agonies." On this view "the rest" would interrupt the one kindly man, grudging Christ even a moment's relief.

Ellicott, however, hears "eager expectation rather than derision": "Was the great and dreadful day (Malachi 4:5) about to burst on them? Would the long-expected prophet at last appear? The sponge and vinegar would seem to minds thus on the stretch an unworthy interruption of the catastrophe of the great drama of which they were spectators." Robertson splits the difference: "the excuse had a pious sound as they misunderstood the words of Jesus," noting the rare future participle (sōsōn, "to save") expressing purpose. Whether mockery or expectation, the bystanders wished to wait and watch for an Elijah who would not come.

The witnesses also note a textual point. Clarke and Robertson record that several ancient manuscripts add here the words from John 19:34 — "Another, taking a spear, pierced his side, and there came out blood and water" — but both reject them as an interpolation: Robertson observes that the addition "makes complete wreck of the context, for in verse 50 Jesus cried with a loud voice and was not yet dead in verse 49," judging it "a crass mechanical copying by some scribe from John." So the witnesses agree the genuine text leaves the Sufferer hanging amid a crowd that waited, half in scorn and half in dread, for a deliverer who would not descend — for the true deliverance was being wrought not by Elijah's coming but by the Son's dying, in the very moment they thought to watch for a sign. The genuine text, the expositors agree, leaves the Sufferer hanging amid a crowd that waited, half in scorn and half in dread, for a deliverer who would never descend, while the true deliverance was being wrought in the very dying they thought to interrupt.
50:
"Jesus, when he had cried again with a loud voice, yielded up the ghost." The witnesses dwell on the loud cry and on the reality and voluntariness of the death. The Puritan Henry expounds the loud cry as the sign of an undiminished life freely surrendered: "the voice of dying men is one of the first things that fails... but Christ, just before he expired, spoke like a man in his full strength, to show that his life was not forced from him, but was freely delivered by him into his Father's hands, as his own act and deed." He that could cry thus "could have got loose from the arrest he was under, and have bid defiance to the powers of death; but to show that by the eternal Spirit he offered himself, being the Priest as well as the Sacrifice, he cried with a loud voice." The cry, Henry adds, signified that he "attacked our spiritual enemies with undaunted courage," spoiling principalities and powers (Isaiah 63:1), like Samson bowing with all his might (Judges 16:30).

On "yielded up the ghost," the witnesses stress the genuine death. Henry: "the Son of God upon the cross did truly and properly die by the violence of the pain he was put to; his soul was separated from his body, and so his body was left really and truly dead." It was "requisite that he should die," for "the Mediator of the new covenant must make atonement by means of death; otherwise no remission" (Hebrews 9:15). Gill notes the two sayings Luke and John record at this moment — "Father, into thy hands I commend my Spirit" and "It is finished."

The Latin Augustine (on the cry) marvels at the divine love: Christ "came from the bosom of the Father to take upon Him our death, that we might be set free by His death; by despising death He exalted us mortals." The grammatical-historical Robertson and the Reformed Calvin keep the central point that the loud cry proved he died not by mere exhaustion but by a free laying-down of life, "no man taketh it from me" (John 10:18). So the witnesses make the death at once truly real — a genuine separation of soul and body, the price of redemption paid — and truly voluntary, the Priest offering himself the Sacrifice, the loud cry the trumpet over the one all-sufficient offering.
51:
"And, behold, the veil of the temple was rent in twain from the top to the bottom; and the earth did quake, and the rocks rent." The witnesses read the rending veil as the supreme sign of the cross's meaning, and the Puritan Henry gives it four senses. It corresponded to "the temple of Christ's body, now in dissolving"; it signified "the revealing of the mysteries of the Old Testament," the veil of concealment removed (2 Corinthians 3:13); the "uniting of Jew and Gentile, by removing the partition wall" (Ephesians 2:14); and above all "the consecrating of a new and living way to God," for "the rending of it signified that Christ by his death opened a way to God," that "we have boldness to enter into the holiest by that new and living way which he hath consecrated for us through the veil" (Hebrews 10:19-20). Death itself, Henry adds, "is the rending of the veil of flesh which interposes between us and the holy of holies."

The Alexandrian Origen distinguishes two veils: the outer was rent "that the mysteries might be published which had been hid until the Lord's coming," while "when that which is perfect is come" (1 Corinthians 13:10) the second veil too shall be removed, that we may see "the true Ark of the Testament." The Latin fathers read the quaking earth and rent rocks as creation's response: Hilary — "the earth quaked because it was unequal to contain such a body; the rocks rent, for the Word of God that pierces all strong things had penetrated them." Leo bids "the rocks of unbelieving minds be rent" as the rocks were.

The grammatical-historical commentators note that the veil "parted between the holy place and the most holy," rent "by an invisible power" just as the evening sacrifice was offered, the priests themselves perhaps witnesses. So the witnesses make the rent veil the visible declaration of the cross's achievement: the way into God's presence, closed since Eden, thrown open by the death of the great High Priest, who by his own blood entered once for all into the holy place (Hebrews 9:7), and in token of it the temple's barrier was torn from top to bottom, by the hand of God and not of man.
52:
"And the graves were opened; and many bodies of the saints which slept arose." The witnesses receive this exceptional sign with both reverence and careful inquiry. The grammatical-historical commentators note that graves were hewn in rock, so "the rending of the rocks would lay them open" (Barnes), and that "slept" is the common Scriptural word for death (Daniel 12:2; 1 Thessalonians 4:14). Lightfoot insists the rending of the veil and the opening of the graves were "a peculiar miracle" beyond the earthquake, for "it is more proper for an earthquake to break hard things than soft," the rocks being rent "where sepulchres had been built, so that now the gates of the resurrection were thrown open."

Ellicott faces the difficulty candidly: the narrative is "exceptional in its marvellousness, standing without any collateral testimony" elsewhere in the New Testament, and has been treated by some as "a mythical addition" or as meaning merely that "the bodies of the dead were exposed by the earthquake." Yet he argues that "the brevity and simplicity of the statement differences it very widely from such legends," and that those who believe Christ in Hades "proclaimed His victory over death" will not think it impossible "that there should have been outward tokens of such a work." He connects it strikingly with Peter's words that Christ "preached to the spirits in prison" (1 Peter 3:19; 4:6), suggesting the apostle may have seen these risen saints.

The Alexandrian Origen reads it allegorically as well: "the graves are the bodies of sinful souls, dead to God; but when by God's grace these souls have been raised, their bodies which before were graves become bodies of Saints." The Latin Hilary takes the opened graves to mean "the bands of death were loosed," Christ "illumining the darkness of death." Cyril of Jerusalem cites the wonder as proof of the resurrection: "many dead were raised without having even touched Him" (contrast Elisha, 2 Kings 13:21). So the witnesses, weighing the difficulty, hold the sign as a true and singular foretoken of the general resurrection that the death and rising of Christ secured.
53:
"And came out of the graves after his resurrection, and went into the holy city, and appeared unto many." The witnesses fasten on the words "after his resurrection," which fix the order and guard Christ's primacy. The grammatical-historical and Reformed commentators agree, with Gill, that "he rose as the firstfruits, the firstborn from the dead... it was necessary that Christ the firstfruits should rise first" (1 Corinthians 15:20). Clarke notes that "the graves were opened at his death by the earthquake, and the bodies came out at his resurrection," not before, "for Christ was himself the first fruits of them who slept." Barnes leaves open whether they "came to life before Jesus rose and remained in the tombs, or came to life after," judging the latter probable, and confesses "what became of them is not revealed, and conjecture is vain."

On the purpose, Barnes suggests it was "amidst the other wonders attending the death of Jesus, to convince the Jews that he was the Messiah," perhaps "some who had been his open friends raised up as an attestation." Clarke draws Quesnel's reflections — the risen bodies showing that Christ's death was "to destroy the death of sin, and confer the life of grace," and to publish "the eternal happiness of body and soul for all that believe."

The Reformed Calvin comments on "the holy city," which Matthew calls holy "not to applaud the character of its inhabitants" — for it was then "full of all pollution" — but because of "God's adoption, which could not be effaced by any corruptions of men till its rejection was openly declared." Cyril of Jerusalem identifies "the holy city" with "this city in which we now are," and presses the wonder as evidence of the resurrection. So the witnesses, while owning much that is hidden, hold the order firm: Christ rose first as the firstfruits, and these risen saints, appearing in Jerusalem, were living witnesses that his resurrection had broken the power of the grave for all his people. So the witnesses, owning much that is hidden, hold the order firm and the testimony sure: Christ rose first as the firstfruits, and these risen saints, appearing in the city, were living evidence that his rising had broken the grave's power for all his own.
54:
"Now when the centurion, and they that were with him, watching Jesus, saw the earthquake, and those things that were done, they feared greatly, saying, Truly this was the Son of God." The witnesses treat the centurion's confession as the first Gentile fruit of the cross, while weighing how much he meant by it. The grammatical-historical commentators read the words from the centurion's own standpoint. Barnes: "the centurion was a heathen, and had probably no very distinct notions of the phrase the Son of God; perhaps understanding by it only that he was like the heathen heroes who had been deified," yet "he certainly regarded these wonders as proof that he was what he professed to be." Clarke and Ellicott agree the words, with no article in the Greek ("a son of God"), to a Roman "would signify a very eminent or Divine person, a hero"; Luke's softer report, "certainly this was a righteous man," helps fix the sense, and Ellicott compares the contemporary language of Wisdom 2:13-18, where "just man" and "son of God" are interchangeable.

Lightfoot, however, reads it higher: "this was indeed the Messias," the centurion "having learned this from the people by conversing among them, and seeing the miracles which accompanied the death of Christ," acknowledging him "the Messias of whom he had heard so many great things spoken by the Jews." Robertson holds the truth between: "probably he meant more than merely a righteous man," and "if he was won now to trust in Christ, he came as a pagan and, like the robber who believed, was saved."

The Reformed Calvin and the Wesleyan Wesley read the confession as God's vindication: amid the mockery of priests and people, a heathen soldier is moved by the signs to own the crucified as the Son of God, "God thus showing his disapprobation of this bloody tragedy" (Clarke). Cyril of Jerusalem joins it to the witness of the cross. So the witnesses, whether they make the centurion's words a hero's tribute or a true confession, agree on their import: at the very hour of deepest shame, when Israel's rulers reviled him, the first voice of the Gentile world was raised to confess the Son of God, the firstfruits of the nations the cross would gather.
55:
"And many women were there beholding afar off, which followed Jesus from Galilee, ministering unto him." The witnesses set the faithful women against the fled disciples and exalt their constancy. The Reformed Gill points the contrast sharply: "where was Peter, who had declared he would never be offended... and the rest of the disciples who said the same things? None were present excepting John; but many women, those of the weaker sex, were there, which was a rebuke of the former vanity and confidence of the disciples, and of their present cowardice."

The grammatical-historical commentators dwell on the women's faithful love. Barnes: "while multitudes of men joined in the cry, Crucify him, and forsook him in his trying moments, it does not appear that any of his female followers were thus unfaithful. In the midst of all his trials, they adhered to their Redeemer. Never did female constancy shine more brightly." Clarke adds that "to their everlasting honour, these women evidenced more courage and affectionate attachment to their Lord than the disciples did, who had promised to die with him." Robertson, noting that "we have come to expect the women from Galilee to be faithful, last at the Cross and first at the tomb," observes that they "beheld from afar," perhaps because the cross was ringed with soldiers, or, as Clarke suggests, from modesty, since the crucified were naked.

So the witnesses make the verse a quiet tribute to a love that outlasted fear. The "ministering unto him" recalls (with Barnes, citing Luke 8:2-3) their long service in Galilee, providing for his wants; and now, when the apostles had fled and Peter had denied, these women remained within sight of the cross, watching the death they could not prevent. Robertson, declining to "picture the anguish of heart of these noble women," lets the curtain fall "on that saddest of all tragedies as the loyal band stood and looked." The witnesses find in their faithfulness both a rebuke to the boastful and a pattern for all who would follow Christ — a constancy that holds its ground when the strong have fled.
56:
"Among which was Mary Magdalene, and Mary the mother of James and Joses, and the mother of Zebedee's children." The witnesses identify the three named women and untangle the several Marys of the Gospels. The grammatical-historical and Reformed commentators agree that Mary Magdalene was she "out of whom Christ had cast seven devils" (Gill, Barnes, Mark 16:9), called Magdalene from Magdala or, some suggest, from a word for a plaiter of women's hair; that the second Mary was "the mother of James the less and Joses," wife of Cleophas or Alpheus; and that "the mother of Zebedee's children" — James and John — "was Salome" (Barnes, Mark 15:40). Clarke lists "four remarkable Marys" of the Gospels and notes the difficulty of distinguishing them.

Lightfoot canvasses the rabbinic confusions surrounding "Mary the plaiter of women's hair," whom the Talmudists tangle with the mother of Jesus, and weighs whether Mary Magdalene was the same as Mary the sister of Lazarus — a view he and Baronius incline to, though Clarke leaves it doubtful. The expositors are concerned chiefly to fix the identities so that the witnesses to the death, the burial, and the resurrection may be clearly known.

So the witnesses use the verse to anchor the chain of testimony. These named women, the expositors note across this passage, were the same who would mark the place of burial, prepare the spices, and come first to the empty tomb; their presence here, at the cross, makes them eyewitnesses of the whole, from the death to the rising. Wesley and the others keep the identifications plain. The naming of the women, far from incidental, secures the human credibility of the gospel record: not anonymous report but named and known witnesses, who followed from Galilee and beheld the end, stand behind the account of the death of Christ and the empty grave that followed. The naming of the women, far from incidental, secures the human credibility of the record: not anonymous rumour but known and named witnesses, who had followed from Galilee and would mark the tomb and come first to find it empty, stand behind the gospel account of the death and burial of Christ.
57:
"When the even was come, there came a rich man of Arimathaea, named Joseph, who also himself was Jesus' disciple." The witnesses honour Joseph's courage and read his every detail with meaning. The Latin fathers dwell on his character and office. Remigius notes that "he that should have the burial of the Lord's body ought to have been such, that he might be deserving of that office by righteous merit," and reads the linen as signifying that Christ's body, "taken of the earth, that is of a Virgin, through the toil of passion came to the whiteness of immortality." Jerome explains that he is "described as rich, not out of ambition to represent so noble a man as Jesus' disciple, but to show how he was able to obtain the body from Pilate; for poor and unknown individuals would not have dared to approach the representative of Roman power." Chrysostom marvels at his boldness: "he risked his life, and took upon him many enmities... and not only dares to ask for Christ's body, but also to bury it."

The grammatical-historical and Reformed commentators note the providence in a rich disciple coming forward. Robertson and Barnes observe that Joseph, a counsellor of the Sanhedrin (Luke 23:50), now openly avowed the Christ whom others had condemned. The Puritan Henry and the others read in the honourable burial the fulfilment of Isaiah 53:9, "he made his grave with the rich in his death."

The Latin Pseudo-Augustine adds that "the Saviour was laid in a tomb belonging to another man, because He died for the salvation of others." Jerome draws the moral against ostentation: the Lord's body "was wrapped not in gold, jewels, or silk, but in clean linen; and he who wrapped it is he who embraces Jesus with a pure heart." So the witnesses make Joseph the type of a bold and reverent faith: a man of rank and wealth who, when the apostles had fled, came forward at the hour of greatest danger to claim and honour the body of his crucified Lord, and so was found worthy to render the last service to the Son of God.
58:
"He went to Pilate, and begged the body of Jesus. Then Pilate commanded the body to be delivered." The witnesses dwell on the boldness of the request and the law that made it necessary. The Reformed Gill notes that Joseph "went in, as Mark says, boldly; not being ashamed of Christ crucified, or afraid to own him, though he knew he should incur the displeasure, reproach, and persecution of the Jews." The grammatical-historical Lightfoot supplies the legal background: it was unlawful to leave a body hanging overnight (Deuteronomy 21:23), and the executed were to be buried in the council's appointed grave for the condemned; there Jesus "should have been buried, had not Joseph, with a pious boldness, begged of Pilate that he might be more honourably interred" — a request the council, "out of spite to him, would hardly have permitted if they had been asked."

Barnes and Clarke note that "no one had a right to remove the body but the magistrate," and that without Joseph's intervention Jesus, "by the Jewish customs, would have been either burned, or buried in the common place appointed for executed criminals." Ellicott marks Pilate's compliance as of a piece with his earlier conduct: assuming the death "soon after the ninth hour," Joseph "hastened at once to the Prætorium," and Pilate, who "wondered that death should have come so soon" (Mark 15:44), granted the petition — "as far as he can, he will help the friends and not the foes of the righteous Sufferer," tracing in it "a lingering reverence and admiration."

So the witnesses make the verse a study in courage rewarded and providence at work. Joseph's open approach to Pilate, when the disciples had scattered, secured for the body of Christ an honourable burial in place of the dishonour the Jews intended; and Pilate's grant, whatever its mixed motive, removed the body from the reach of his enemies into the keeping of a friend. The very act that exposed Joseph to reproach became the means by which the burial of the Lord was made dignified, witnessed, and secure — another link, the expositors note, in the chain of evidence that the death was real and the tomb known.
59:
"And when Joseph had taken the body, he wrapped it in a clean linen cloth." The witnesses describe the reverent preparation of the body and supply from John the detail of the spices. The grammatical-historical Barnes and Clarke note that John adds the burial "was done with spices" (John 19:40), the Jews using "myrrh, aloes, and other aromatics in large quantities," a mixture "of myrrh and aloes of one hundred pounds' weight" being applied; and that "there not being time properly to embalm the body before the Sabbath," a second embalming was intended by the women (Luke 23:56; 24:1). Ellicott examines the word for "linen cloth," sindōn, "probably of the nature of muslin rather than linen," used by the Egyptians for wrapping their mummies.

The Reformed Calvin notes that the three Evangelists "glance briefly at the burial," saying nothing of the ointments John mentions, but relate enough to show "that Christ was honourably buried"; "there could be no doubt that when Joseph gave up his own tomb to our Lord, he made provision also for suitable magnificence." And this, Calvin adds, "was brought about by the secret providence of God rather than by the premeditated design of men."

So the witnesses make the wrapping of the body the last office of a reverent love, performed in haste against the approaching Sabbath yet with the costly spices of devotion. The clean linen and the abundant myrrh, the expositors note, both honoured the body of the Lord and, providentially, fixed beyond dispute that he was truly dead and truly buried — for a body so handled, washed, spiced, and wrapped could not afterwards be thought merely swooning. The tenderness of Joseph and Nicodemus toward the dead Christ thus served both their love and the gospel's evidence, preparing the body of the Lord for the grave from which, in three days, it would rise incorruptible. The tenderness of Joseph and Nicodemus toward the dead Christ thus served at once their love and the gospel's evidence, for a body so washed, spiced, and wrapped could never afterward be thought merely to have swooned, preparing the Lord's body for the grave from which it would rise.
60:
"And laid it in his own new tomb, which he had hewn out in the rock: and he rolled a great stone to the door of the sepulchre, and departed." The witnesses find in every detail of the burial a providence guarding the resurrection to come. The grammatical-historical commentators stress the new tomb. Barnes: "in this tomb no man had been laid; this was so ordered in the providence of God that there might be no suspicion about his identity when he rose; that it might not be alleged that another person had risen, or that he was raised by touching the bones of some prophet" (as in 2 Kings 13:21). Gill agrees the new tomb was so ordered "for the confirmation of the truth of his resurrection; for had another body been laid there, it might have been said that it was that, and not his, that was raised."

The witnesses also note the fulfilment of prophecy and the security of the rock. Barnes and Clarke see in the rich man's tomb the fulfilment of Isaiah 53:9, "he made his grave with the rich in his death" — the more remarkable because "during his life he was poor." Clarke observes that, "to all human appearance, the body of Christ must have had the same burial-place with the two robbers"; but now "the sacrifice is offered, the atonement made and accepted, he is no longer to be enrolled with the transgressors." And the tomb "hewn out in the rock," with "no way to access it but by the entrance," and closed with "a great stone," made it impossible "for the disciples to steal him away."

JFB and the expositors note that this very security would soon move the priests to seal and watch the tomb, so that "from this circumstance the resurrection of Christ had its fullest evidence, and was put beyond the power of successful contradiction" (Clarke). So the witnesses make the burial in the rich man's new, rock-hewn, stone-sealed tomb a masterpiece of providence: every circumstance that love and fear contrived to honour or secure the body became, in the event, an unanswerable proof that the One laid there dead was the same One who rose.
61:
"And there was Mary Magdalene, and the other Mary, sitting over against the sepulchre." The witnesses note that the faithful women, who had watched the death, now watched the burial, marking the place against the dawn. The Reformed Calvin draws on the parallels: Matthew and Mark relate only that the women sat by the tomb, "but Luke states their resolution, which was, that they returned to the city, that two days afterwards they might render due honour to the burial." Hence, he says, "their minds were filled with a better odour, which the Lord breathed into his death, that he might bring them to his grave and exalt them higher."

The grammatical-historical Ellicott reads the same constancy: the women "remained by the cross while the body was taken down, and watched its entombment; then, returning to the house, they prepared their spices before the Sabbath began, so that they might be ready by the earliest hour of dawn on the first day of the week" (Luke 23:56). Clarke dwells on their sorrowing love: "these holy women, filled with that love to their Lord which death cannot destroy, cleaved to him in life, and in death were not divided. They came to the grave to see the end, and, overwhelmed with sorrow, sat down to mourn." He notes that the mother of the Lord "had probably by this time been taken home to the house of John" (John 19:27).

The Latin Remigius (on the burial) had already drawn the spiritual lesson: "the women alone, who had loved Him more attachedly, adhered to Him, and with anxious care noted the place where the Lord's body was laid, that at fit time they might perform the service of their devotion." So the witnesses make the watching women the indispensable link between the burial and the resurrection: they marked the very tomb in which he was laid, so that when they came at dawn and found it empty, there could be no mistaking the place. Their patient, sorrowing love, sitting over against the sepulchre, was overruled to be the first and surest testimony that the grave they saw closed they would find open.
62:
"Now the next day, that followed the day of the preparation, the chief priests and Pharisees came together unto Pilate." The witnesses note the priests' restless malice, pursuing Christ even beyond death, and they read the providence in it. The Latin Jerome marks the persistence: "it was not enough for the Chief Priests to have crucified the Lord, if they did not guard the sepulchre, and do their utmost to lay hands on Him as He rose." Hilary calls their fear and their sealing "marks of folly and unbelief, that they should have sought to seal up the tomb of One at whose bidding they had seen a dead man raised."

The Latin Raban explains "the Parasceve" as the preparation for the Sabbath, and draws the parallel of the days: "on the sixth day man was made, and on the seventh God rested; therefore on the sixth day Jesus died for man, and rested the Sabbath day in the tomb." He turns the priests' slander against them: they call Christ "a deceiver" who was "not from truth into error, but leading men from error to truth, from death to life." Remigius and Augustine discuss how Christ rose "after three days," reckoning the parts of three days and nights as whole, and Augustine sees in the three days "the consent of the whole Trinity in the passion of the Son."

The Antiochene Chrysostom draws the great irony, which the Puritan Henry and the grammatical-historical commentators all echo: "observe how against their will they concert to demonstrate the truth, for by their precautions irrefragable demonstration of the resurrection was attained. The sepulchre was watched, and so no fraud could have been practised; and if there was no collusion, it is certain that the Lord rose again." So the witnesses make the priests' plotting, like Pilate's title and the soldiers' watch, an unwitting servant of the gospel: their very effort to bury the truth with the body of Christ became the means by which his resurrection was placed beyond the reach of doubt. So the witnesses make the priests' restless plotting, like Pilate's title and the soldiers' watch, an unwitting servant of the gospel, their very effort to bury the truth with the body becoming the means by which the resurrection was set beyond the reach of doubt.
63:
"Sir, we remember that that deceiver said, while he was yet alive, After three days I will rise again." The witnesses note with irony that the enemies of Christ remembered his prediction which the disciples forgot. The grammatical-historical Robertson presses the point: it is objected "that the Jewish rulers would know nothing of such a prediction, but in Matthew 12:40 he expressly made it to them." He rejects Meyer's view that the whole prophecy is "unhistorical legend," for that would "make legendary much of the Gospels and limit Jesus to a mere man." The real "problem remains why the disciples forgot and the Jewish leaders remembered" — due, he suggests, "to the overwhelming grief of the disciples coupled with the blighting of all their hopes," while suspicion sharpened the rulers' memory. Ellicott agrees: "sorrow and disappointment confuse, and suspicion sharpens the intellect."

The witnesses also weigh the title "that deceiver." Barnes notes "by this title they still chose to designate him, thinking that his death had fully confirmed the truth of the charges." Clarke draws the lesson that "while these wicked men are fulfilling their own vicious counsels, they are subserving the great cause of Christianity," for "everything depended on the resurrection of Christ," and it "was necessary that they should make use of every precaution to prevent an imposture, that the resurrection might have the fullest evidence."

Ellicott observes that the priests' words betray that "they themselves had understood, wholly or in part, the true meaning" of the saying about the temple (John 2:19), being "more clear-sighted than the disciples" — a sharpness he attributes to suspicion. Cyril of Jerusalem, citing the very words, finds in the sealed tomb "a fountain sealed" (Song of Songs 4:12). So the witnesses make the verse a study in the strange workings of providence: the rulers, calling Christ a deceiver and meaning only to guard against fraud, preserved and publicised the very prophecy of the resurrection, and took the very steps that would make its fulfilment undeniable. So the rulers, calling Christ a deceiver and meaning only to guard against fraud, preserved and publicised the very prophecy of the resurrection and took the very steps that would render its fulfilment undeniable, their malice overruled to serve the truth they hated.
64:
"Command therefore that the sepulchre be made sure until the third day, lest his disciples come by night, and steal him away, and say unto the people, He is risen from the dead: so the last error shall be worse than the first." The witnesses note that the priests, in guarding against a fraud they imagined, anticipated and forestalled the very objection their successors would raise. The grammatical-historical commentators observe that the request itself concedes much: "by this they own that he was buried, and knew in what and whose sepulchre he was laid" (Gill). Barnes draws a chronological point from "until the third day": "this proves that when the Jews spoke of three days they did not necessarily mean three whole days, but parts of three days, as was the case in our Saviour's lying in the grave."

On "the last error worse than the first," the commentators explain the two "errors" the priests had in mind. Robertson: "the first fraud was belief in the Messiahship of Jesus, the second belief in his resurrection"; Barnes: the last deception "shall have a wider influence among the people than the first." Ellicott connects the word with the "deceiver" of the previous verse, the priests fearing a continuing "deceit."

Cyril of Jerusalem, citing the request, sees the sealed tomb prefigured as "a fountain sealed" (Song of Songs 4:12), the Saviour himself being the fountain of living water. So the witnesses make the verse a remarkable instance of God overruling the malice of men. The priests, fearing that the disciples might steal the body and claim a resurrection, demanded the very guard and seal that would make such theft impossible and the true resurrection unanswerable. Their own words framed the false hypothesis — a stolen body — and their own precautions destroyed it in advance; so that the objection they raised against the resurrection became, by the watch they set, the strongest argument for its truth. So the priests, framing with their own lips the false hypothesis of a stolen body, and demanding with their own request the guard that would make such theft impossible, destroyed their objection in advance, so that the watch they set became the strongest argument for the resurrection they feared.
65:
"Pilate said unto them, Ye have a watch: go your way, make it as sure as ye can." The witnesses weigh whether Pilate granted a Roman guard or referred them to their own temple police, and they read his curt reply. The grammatical-historical Robertson and Ellicott take the words as an imperative — "Take ye a guard" — of Roman soldiers, the Latin term custodia being used; "if the priests had had such a guard at their disposal before, there would have been no need for them to apply to Pilate" (Ellicott). Robertson hears the governor's contempt: "the curt permission to the Jews whom he despised is suitable in the mouth of the Roman official — make it secure for yourselves as you know how." Clarke and Gill, however, suppose he gave them leave to employ the Roman cohort that guarded the temple (Acts 4:1; Luke 22:4), "some of these companies, not then on duty," to watch the tomb.

The Reformed Calvin reads the permission as binding the priests the more firmly: "this permission bound them more firmly, so that they could not escape by any evasion; for though they were not ashamed to break out against Christ after his resurrection, yet with Pilate's signet they as truly shut their own mouths as they shut up the sepulchre." Wesley simply notes "a guard of your own, in the tower of Antonia, which was stationed there for the service of the temple."

So the witnesses, whether the guard was Pilate's soldiers or the temple cohort under his leave, agree on the effect: an official, armed watch was set over the tomb with Pilate's authority, so that the priests themselves became responsible for its security. The very thoroughness of their precaution — soldiers, a seal, a sealed stone — would, in the event, leave them without excuse; for when the tomb was found empty despite the guard, their own measures testified that no human hand had opened it. Pilate's careless "make it as sure as ye can" handed them a security that would convict, not clear, them. So the very thoroughness of their precaution — soldiers, a seal, a sealed stone, all by Pilate's authority — would in the event leave them without excuse, for a tomb so secured and yet found empty testified that no human hand had opened it.
66:
"So they went, and made the sepulchre sure, sealing the stone, and setting a watch." The witnesses close the chapter by gathering up the great theme they have traced throughout — that the enemies of Christ, in their utmost effort to bury the truth, secured its proof. The grammatical-historical Barnes describes the sealing: the stone fastened "in such a way that it could not be removed without detection," perhaps as Daniel's den was sealed "with the king's signet" (Daniel 6:17), by cords and wax impressed with the seal of Pilate or of the priests. Clarke construes the Greek to mean they "made the tomb secure by the guard and by sealing the stone," the guard preventing theft and the seal preventing the guard's corruption.

Barnes draws out the providence at length: "we cannot but be struck with the wisdom of God in ordering the circumstances of the Saviour's burial so as to avoid the possibility of deception. Had all this been done by his friends, it might have been said that they only pretended he was dead. But he was adjudged to be dead by the Jews themselves; Pilate was satisfied of it; they had their own way about his burial; he was buried alone; the place was made sure expressly to prevent his being removed; and they placed around him a guard, in their own judgment large enough." Thus "his very enemies took every possible precaution to place his resurrection beyond the possibility of suspicion of fraud."

Clarke marvels at the same overruling: "how wonderful are the wisdom and goodness of God! every thing was done which human policy and prudence could to prevent a resurrection, which these very precautions had the most direct tendency to authenticate and establish." JFB, Wesley and Cyril of Jerusalem echo the theme — Cyril citing the "fountain sealed" of Song of Songs 4:12. So the witnesses end the account of the burial on a note of triumphant irony: the sealed stone and the armed watch, contrived by malice and granted by Pilate, became the strongest of all witnesses to the resurrection, for a tomb so secured, found empty on the third day, could be explained by no power but the power of God who raised his Son.
